llm_traces.reason_for_request: 从目录页截图中抽取单页原子目录项
llm_traces.request_prompt: 你会看到一页书籍目录截图。请只提取真正可导航的目录项，并按从上到下的顺序返回 JSON 数组。每项格式必须是 [{"title":"章节标题","depth":0,"printed_page":12,"visual_order":1,"role_hint":"content","endnotes_candidate":false,"endnotes_subentry_candidate":false}]。规则：1. depth 只做单页粗粒度层级判断：顶层正文/前后附记为 0，容器如 Part/Book/COURS/APPENDICES/INDICES/Notes 可为 1，章内子项可更深；2. role_hint 只能是 container/content/back_matter 之一；3. container 只用于组织层标题，如 Part、Book、COURS、APPENDICES、INDICES、Notes；它们不是正文 chapter；4. content 包括正文 chapter、章内 section，以及 front_matter/post_body 这类有页码的可导航条目；back_matter 只用于 Bibliography、References、Index、Note on Sources（单行独立）这类参考性材料；5. 目录页总标题如 Contents、Sommaire、Table des matieres 不输出；标题下面很长的摘要、说明文字、介绍段落不能输出；纯数字孤立行不要单独输出；5. 没有可见页码就把 printed_page 设为 null，绝不猜测；6. Notes、Endnotes、Notes on the Text、Notes to the Chapters、Chapter Notes 这类尾注容器可额外标 endnotes_candidate=true；7. Notes to Chapter 1、Notes to Introduction、或尾注容器下按章分组的子条目可额外标 endnotes_subentry_candidate=true；8. 不要补全不可见文字，只做空白归一化。

llm_traces.reason_for_request: 根据整份目录页重建目录树，并识别尾注容器与子项
llm_traces.request_prompt:
你会依次看到一整份书籍目录页截图。请按整份目录的真实组织方式输出 JSON 对象，不要只做扁平标题提取。输出格式必须是 {"endnotes_summary":{"present":false,"container_title":null,"container_printed_page":null,"container_visual_order":null,"has_chapter_keyed_subentries_in_toc":false,"subentry_pattern":null},"items":[{"title":"章节标题","depth":0,"visual_order":1,"printed_page":12,"role_hint":"chapter","parent_title":"Part One"}]}。规则：1. 必须保留无页码的容器层，例如 Part、Book、COURS、INDICES、APPENDICES；这些条目是 container，不是 chapter。2. chapter 是正文里最独立的可导航单元：显式编号章，以及有正文页码的 Introduction、Conclusion、Préambule、单独 Epilogue。chapter 挂在容器下时仍然是 chapter，不会因为容器存在而变成 section。3. front_matter 只用于第一条正文 chapter 之前的辅助材料，如 Acknowledgments、Remerciements、Preface、Foreword、List of Abbreviations、List of Illustrations。4. back_matter 只用于书末参考性材料，如 Bibliography、References、Index、Indices、Works Cited，以及单行独立的 Note on Sources。5. post_body 只用于最后一条正文 chapter 之后、back_matter 之前的实质性内容，如 RÉSUMÉ、SITUATION、Postambule、独立带页码的 Appendix。6. 书末独立的 Notes、Endnotes、Notes on the Text、Notes to the Chapters、Chapter Notes 是 endnotes 容器，不改变正文 chapter 的层级基准；Bibliography、References、Index 永远不要标成 endnotes。7. Note on Sources 只有在下方有按章 notes 子项时才允许判成 endnotes；如果只是单行独立条目，固定判成 back_matter。8. 如果目录里列出了尾注容器下按章分组的子条目，这些子条目标成 section，parent_title 写尾注容器标题。9. 目录页总标题如 Contents、Sommaire 不输出；标题下方的说明性斜体副标题、摘要句、时间说明如果不是独立可导航标题，不输出。10. parent_title 只写直接父标题；最顶层为空字符串；没有可见页码就把 printed_page 设为 null；请保持从前到后、从上到下的视觉顺序。已抽到的目录标题草稿如下，请优先沿用可见标题原文和顺序，但可以补回漏掉的容器层：

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

cluster.chapter_title: From Wind to mucus and Fire The innovations of Jin and Yuan physicians, ca. 1150-1360

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Liu Wansu used the fluid interactions between the Five Agents as a guide for scripting both physiological and pathological processes. $ ^{8} $ The Five Agents and the five Yin viscera (五臟) interacted in a way illustrated synoptically in Figure 4.1 (next page). This system was homeostatic (it had an inherent tendency to equilibrium) but dynamic. Since all of its elements were inter-related, a slight imbalance (be it excess or deficiency) in any of the Five Agents or organs led to illnesses that could affect the entire organism. By a sequence of effects predicted by the model, an excess of emotions could, for instance, lead to hemiplegia:

Using the compelling conceptual architecture of the wuyun liuqi, the Yuanbing shi seemed to make sense of everything: why vomit is acidic, why we have runny noses, why plump persons suffer from more strokes, why some people speak in their sleep, why we laugh when we are tickled, and even why we don't laugh when we tickle ourselves. $ ^{10} $

mechanisms of illness”聞病機何如. $ ^{11} $ Qibo traced one illness each to the five Yin viscera, one each to the lower and upper body, and twelve to various aspects of Qi: five (including kuang) to Fire, four to Heat, and one each to Wind, Dampness, and Cold.

was not air, but an internal form of Qi that appeared mostly because of internally generated Heat and acted in concert with Heat to produce morbid symptoms. As he would state in a later work, “what we speak of as ‘Wind’ is [in fact] ‘Heat’” (凡言風者熱也), because “Wind originally is born from Heat; Heat is the root, Wind the top branch [the superficial manifestation]” (風本生於熱 以熱為本 以風為標). $ ^{14} $

A reader of Liu's treatise would have had no inkling that madness had once been classified as a "Wind illness." Following the passage from the Basic Questions that he was commenting upon, Liu discussed madness under the "Fire" section, where he gave kuang a strong moral meaning: "kuang' means wild unruliness, with no correct or fixed [behavior]; 'yuè' means violating rites and laws and having lost one's normal state" (狂者 狂亂而無正定 越者 乖越禮法而失常也). $ ^{15} $

onset of madness as follows: “Kidney-Water governs the Will, and Water and Fire are opposed to each other. Therefore when Cardiac Fire is overabundant, Renal Water decays: one thus ‘loses one’s Will’ and becomes crazy” (臀水主志而水火相反 故心火旺則腎水衰 乃失志而狂越). $ ^{16} $ Liu also thought that excessive emotions caused by Heat or Fire could trigger madness. $ ^{17} $

Liu Wansu evaluated at some length two sources that disagreed on the nature of the association of dian and kuang: the $ 20^{th} $ problem of the Canon of Problems, and one of Wang Bing's annotations to the Basic Questions (see 3.4). $ ^{18} $ Since neither the $ 20^{th} $ problem nor Wang Bing's commentary mentioned the symptoms of dian and kuang, Liu Wansu could adduce or dispute either passage no matter what meaning he gave to dian. His interpretation of dian and kuang as two similar disorders was widely adopted in Ming medicine, when the great seventh- and eighth-century treatises that had described dian as an epileptiform illness had stopped being reprinted.

It is said that ‘the doubling of Yang is kuang and the doubling of Yin is dian’, but this differs from the explanation found in the Basic Questions. The Canon’s annotations say:¹⁹ ‘excessive joy makes dian, excessive anger makes kuang.’ Joy is the emotion associated with the Heart: when Heat in the Heart is overabundant, there is excessive joy, creating dian. Anger is the emotion associated with the Liver. When Fire is overreplete it curbs Metal, which cannot appease Wood [the Agent associated with the Liver]. Therefore the Liver becomes over-replete: one is excessively angry, creating

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: As Zhang remarked, this passage failed to reveal what illnesses were triggered by these disorderly movements of Qi. He complemented this lack with one passage from the Divine Pivot (pian 8) that explained the effects of emotions on the viscera: ratiocination and pensiveness (思慮), associated with the Heart, harmed the spirit (神); sorrow (悲哀; Liver), harmed the bone souls (魄); joy (喜樂; Lungs), the cloud souls (魂); despondency (愁憂; Spleen), intent or consciousness (意); whereas fear, associated with no particular organ, harmed refined Qi, or “essence” (精). Since this passage discussed neither Cold and Heat nor fright and fatigue, Zhang associated each of the nine forms of Qi defined in pian 39 of Basic Questions with the illnesses that the Divine Pivot claimed they could trigger.

Anger could thus overcome excessive pensiveness, the affect associated with the Spleen. $ ^{52} $ Anger could itself be assuaged by sorrow, the emotion correlated with the Lungs and Metal (the agent that was said to conquer Wood). The two forms of Qi that did not fit into this scheme were fatigue, which should be soothed with idleness (yi 逸), and fright, which was countered by habit (xi 習). $ ^{53} $

What Charlotte Furth has called the “art of diagnosis” is best understood as the art of designing prescriptions on the basis of case-specific diagnoses. $ ^{67} $ The flexible articulation of a prescription to a diagnosis is so often treated as a natural feature of “traditional Chinese medicine” that its historical origins are usually ignored. $ ^{68} $ Zhang and his disciples were in fact among the first to put this principle at the center of their doctrines. $ ^{69} $ Before them, the default mode of prescription (typified by the Song formularies that became their favorite foils) had been to prescribe ready-made remedies for predefined illnesses. Zhang Yuansu and his followers criticized contemporaries for not distinguishing between Cold and Heat or between depletion and repletion, and for consequently failing to prescribe the right remedies in specific illness cases. $ ^{70} $ They also criticized books like the Recipes of the Pharmacy Service,

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Li Gao 李杲 (1180-1251) was born in a prominent clan from Zhending 真定 (also in Hebei). It is said that Li studied the healing arts because his mother had died at the hands of incompetent physicians. He gave Zhang Yuansu, who was then renowned in the Yan 燕 and Zhao 趙 regions, a hefty sum of money to learn medicine from him. $ ^{71} $ With Zhang, Li studied the Inner Canon, the Treatise on Cold Injury, the Canon of Problems, and probably Zhang's digest of these works, Instructions on the Origins of Medical Learning (Yixue qiyuan 醫學啟源). $ ^{72} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Li Gao 李杲 (1180-1251) was born in a prominent clan from Zhending 真定 (also in Hebei). It is said that Li studied the healing arts because his mother had died at the hands of incompetent physicians. He gave Zhang Yuansu, who was then renowned in the Yan 燕 and Zhao 趙 regions, a hefty sum of money to learn medicine from him. $ ^{71} $ With Zhang, Li studied the Inner Canon, the Treatise on Cold Injury, the Canon of Problems, and probably Zhang's digest of these works, Instructions on the Origins of Medical Learning (Yixue qiyuan 醫學啟源). $ ^{72} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: became the mainstay of Liu's most influential book: the Method for Tracing Illnesses to their Origins with the Mysterious Mechanism of the Basic Questions (Suwen xuanji yuanbing shi 素問玄機原 病式; hereafter Yuanbing shi).⁷ There, Liu alleged that “in the method for distinguishing illnesses, it can be seen clearly that the Qi of [all] illnesses can be reduced to transformations of the five phases and six Qi” (識病之法 以其病氣歸於五運六氣之化 明可見矣). Liu Wansu used the fluid interactions between the Five Agents as a guide for scripting both physiological and pathological processes. $ ^{8} $ The Five Agents and the five Yin viscera (五臟) interacted in a way illustrated synoptically in Figure 4.1 (next page). This system was homeostatic (it had an inherent tendency to equilibrium) but dynamic. Since all of its elements were inter-related, a slight imbalance (be it excess or deficiency) in any of the Five Agents or organs led to illnesses that could affect the entire organism. By a sequence of effects predicted by the model, an excess of emotions could, for instance, lead to hemiplegia: Hemiplegia caused by Wind strokes [occurs] because Cardiac Fire abruptly becomes excessive, and [Kidney] Water decays and is unable to curb it. Fire can thus overcome Metal, as a result of which Metal can no longer overcome Wood: Liver Wood thus becomes excessive of its own and joins with the heat of the Fire, causing [th ...

By explaining illness on the plane of the Five Agents, Liu severed the causal chain from the organs themselves.
Fig. 4.1 Mutual conquest and mutual production cycles of the Five Agents and their associated viscera. (This diagram represents the system in a balanced state.)
Using the compelling conceptual architecture of the wuyun liuqi, the Yuanbing shi seemed to make sense of everything: why vomit is acidic, why we have runny noses, why plump persons suffer from more strokes, why some people speak in their sleep, why we laugh when we are tickled, and even why we don't laugh when we tickle ourselves. $ ^{10} $ Liu's ambitious treatise was in fact an extensive commentary on a passage from chapter 74 of the Basic Questions (one of the seven "Grand treatises") where Qibo explained the origins of 19 disorders to satisfy the Yellow Emperor's wish to "hear about the

mechanisms of illness”聞病機何如. $ ^{11} $ Qibo traced one illness each to the five Yin viscera, one each to the lower and upper body, and twelve to various aspects of Qi: five (including kuang) to Fire, four to Heat, and one each to Wind, Dampness, and Cold. Extrapolating that the five illnesses the canonical excerpt attributed to the viscera actually originated in the movements of Fire, Liu Wansu took this passage as authoritative proof of the pathological power of Heat and Fire, which now represented 14 out of the original 19 entries—five from the viscera, four from Heat, and five from Fire. The short first section on “the five circulatory phases [as they] govern illness” (五運主病) (1a-3b) discussed the Five Agents as they succeeded each other during the year (Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, and Water). The rest of the work was devoted to “the six Qi [as they] bring about illness” (六氣為病). Heat (4b-31a) and Fire (33a-57b) made up almost three quarters of the entire book. $ ^{12} $ Liu took the six Qi not as climatic influences (their traditional meaning), but largely as manifestations of internal imbalances. The close attention he paid to internal pathological processes makes him representative of a larger trend in Chinese medical thinking that I started to explore in the previous chapter when I showed that Song physicians increasingly explained Wind illnesses by internal processes govern ...

was not air, but an internal form of Qi that appeared mostly because of internally generated Heat and acted in concert with Heat to produce morbid symptoms. As he would state in a later work, “what we speak of as ‘Wind’ is [in fact] ‘Heat’” (凡言風者熱也), because “Wind originally is born from Heat; Heat is the root, Wind the top branch [the superficial manifestation]” (風本生於熱 以熱為本 以風為標). $ ^{14} $ Liu Wansu's descriptions of the pathogenesis of a large number of illnesses gave a central place to internal Fire and Heat (instead of Wind). Liu's focus on Heat also explains his predilection for the use of cooling drugs, many of which he drew from the Treatise on Cold Injury. Liu's reinterpretation of Wind as inner Heat contributed to the collapse of the notion of "Wind illnesses" (see sections 4.2, 4.4, and 4.7 below), which in turn helped kuang to become one among many "miscellaneous illnesses" (雜病) that could be discussed independently. A reader of Liu's treatise would have had no inkling that madness had once been classified as a "Wind illness." Following the passage from the Basic Questions that he was commenting upon, Liu discussed madness under the "Fire" section, where he gave kuang a strong moral meaning: "kuang' means wild unruliness, with no correct or fixed [behavior]; 'yuè' means violating rites and laws and having lost one's normal state" (狂者 狂亂而無正定 越者 乖越禮法而失常也). $ ^{15} $ I ...

onset of madness as follows: “Kidney-Water governs the Will, and Water and Fire are opposed to each other. His interpretation of dian and kuang as two similar disorders was widely adopted in Ming medicine, when the great seventh- and eighth-century treatises that had described dian as an epileptiform illness had stopped being reprinted. It is said that ‘the doubling of Yang is kuang and the doubling of Yin is dian’, but this differs from the explanation found in the Basic Questions. The Canon’s annotations say:¹⁹ ‘excessive joy makes dian, excessive anger makes kuang.’ Joy is the emotion associated with the Heart: when Heat in the Heart is overabundant, there is excessive joy, creating dian. Anger is the ...

kuang. Since all five emotions generate Heat, kuang is triggered by any of the five emotions. It is simply that anger is [a] more common [cause]. $ ^{20} $ 或云重陽者狂 重陰者癲 則素問之說不同也 經注曰 多喜為癲 多怒為狂 然喜為心志 故心熱甚則多喜而為癲也 怒為肝志 火實制金不能平木故肝實則多怒而為狂也 況五志所發皆為熱 故狂者五志間發 但怒多爾 After having cited many passages from the Basic Questions that were compatible with his own view of emotions as manifestations of Fire and of madness as caused by inner Heat, Liu concluded with a rhetorical question aimed to refute the Canon of Problems: “Can it really be, then, that dian is the doubling of Yin?” (夫豈癲為重陰者歟) Liu Wansu's interpretations were not grounded in his (or anybody's) observation of patients. His reasoning relied on textual authority and on deduction following the association of the five viscera with the Five Agents and the emotions. Bypassing the entire "Wind illness" tradition, Liu confirmed his Heat etiology by citing passages from the Treatise on Cold Injury—another work he greatly admired—that attributed disorders of the Yang-brilliance tract to Heat. $ ^{21} $ I know of no major medical work after Liu's Yuanbing shi 原病式 that attributed madness to Wind, whereas Heat and Fire remained important identified causes of psycho-behavioral disorders until the end of imperial history. Later physicians also took Liu Wansu's comparison of the Canon of Problems with Wang Bing's later annotation as an argument fo ...

kuang in symptoms (instead of an epileptiform illness) took place only in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, a process I shall describe in more detail in sections 6.5 and 6.6. $ ^{22} $ ### 4.2 Zhang Congzheng: convergences and crystallizations Zhang Congzheng 張從正 (1156-1228), from Kaocheng 考城 in Suizhou 睢州 (Henan), was the first Chinese physician to attribute madness to Heat and mucus explicitly and to treat it consistently with purgatives and emetics. He also systematized counter-affect therapy, a kind of treatment that came to be discussed next to madness in the sixteenth century (6.4). cluster.chapter_title: “Danxi said…” The textual life of medical ideas, 1360-1530

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Zbezbong is difficult to translate. One of the earliest meanings of zbe 折 was “to break” or “to snap.” In legal language, zbe yu 折獄 meant “judging [i.e., deciding] a criminal case.” $ ^{6} $ But zbe in zbe yu also referred to the weighing and pondering that led to the final decision. $ ^{7} $ Zbe zbong (折中 or 折衷) itself implied the balanced assessment of various positions. $ ^{8} $ We may gloss zbezbong as to resolve—a case, a point of doctrine, etc.—by arbitrating between various positions, often on the basis of another authority. $ ^{9} $

Intertextual analysis also reveals why medical knowledge was not simply “passed on” from one generation to the other. It shows that the illness scripts that interest us were more than culturally informed responses to observed symptoms. These scripts also emerged and developed intertextually. Finally, the zbezzhong mode of interpreting the past largely explains why Chinese medical thinking often appears so “syncretistic” and “integrative.” $ ^{10} $

The result of zbezzhong writing was not a simple average of previous teachings, it was a coherent amalgamation of several approaches to illness. Zbezzhong was deliberate syncretism—-eclecticism by design, not by default. Focusing on the intertextuality inherent in the zbezzhong approach avoids treating changes in medical doctrine as radical departures from predecessors or as the unfolding of realizations in the consciousness of a series of unrelated authors. Intertextual analysis also reveals why medical knowledge was not simply “passed on” from one generation to the other. It shows that the illness scripts that interest us were more than culturally informed responses to observed symptoms. These scripts also emerged and developed intertextually. Finally, the zbezzhong mode of interpreting the past largely explains why Chinese medical thinking often appears so “syncretistic” and “integrative.” $ ^{10} $

This chapter explores the way medical texts were composed and handed down, the history of important medical doctrines, and the formation and transmission of medical ideas on madness. I first analyze the most elaborate section on madness of all pre-1500 Chinese medical treatises—the section on kuang of a book by Zhu Zhenheng's disciple Lou Ying 櫻英 (1320-1400)—to show how an erudite physician selected from a rich textual heritage to construct a coherent view of illness. I then discuss how another illustrious disciple of Zhu's, Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405), contributed to giving prestige to the Danxi approach. Next, I explain how two books that Liu Chun 劉純 (1340-1412), whose father had been Zhu's pupil, compiled in Shaanxi in the late fourteenth century typified the zhezhong approach to the past and further helped to diffused Danxi medicine. In a fourth section, I explain the emergence of the core works of the Danxi corpus in the late fifteenth century and the evolution of their sections on madness. The final two parts are about two writers whose works based on Danxi texts were widely debated and cited in the decades that followed their death. These writers were Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510), from Ningbo, and Yu Tuan 虞搏 (1438-1517), from the same locale as Zhu in Zhejiang. Though I focus on individual writers, I insist that they were responding to each other and explain how each of them proposed different interpretations of a common body of texts that included the Danxi corpus, but also the Inner Canon and the works of Liu Wansu and Li Gao. $ ^{11} $ Throughout, I try to recreate the doctrinal debates that make changes in their approaches to madness intelligible.

The only early-Ming treatise that contained a substantial section on madness was Lou Ying's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning (Yixue gangmu). This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the "Immortals' crag" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $

This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. The only early-Ming treatise that contained a substantial section on madness was Lou Ying's Systematic Outline of Medical Learning (Yixue gangmu). This work exemplifies how a highly literate practitioner could devise approaches to madness from the rich array of texts that were available to him. Lou Ying lived at the "Immortals' crag" (仙岩) in Xiaoshan 蕭山, a town located southeast of Hangzhou across the Qiantang River on an extension of the Grand Canal. Probably introduced by his father Lou Youxian 友賢, Lou Ying studied medicine under Zhu Zhenheng's guidance. $ ^{13} $ In 1362, a little less than four years after he attended Zhu's funeral, Lou opened a school where he received disciples. $ ^{14} $ He became famous enough to be summoned to Nanjing in 1377 and 1381 to treat the emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, of the newly established Ming dynasty), but he refused permanent employment at court both times. $ ^{15} $

Another reason why Lou took so long to complete his opus was the complexity of its subject matter. Lou opened his preface with a telling statement on “medicine as a learning” (醫之為學): “its Way is extensive, its meaning profound, its books infinitely abundant” (其道博 其義深 其書浩瀚). $ ^{16} $ Although Lou claimed that the essentials of medicine “simply hinge on Yin-Yang and the Five Agents” (其要不過陰陽五行而已), he admitted he took more than thirty years to realize that the myriad transformations of illness emerged from imbalances in Yin-Yang and the Five Agents. $ ^{17} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: appended one or two recipes under each illness. $ ^{80} $ Zhao had first seen the Recorded Sayings in the Suzhou area some years after Zhu's death in 1358, and again ten years later at many friends' places (遍見於朋友處), under a new title: Essentials of Danxi Remedies (Danxi yaoyao 丹溪藥要). Lamenting that this book was of such low quality, yet was circulating widely by virtue of its attribution (欲駕其名以傳諸徒 豈先生之意哉), Zhao decided to re-edit it in a question-and-answer format and to enhance it with his own comments. The result was Questions on the Essentials of Danxi Remedies (Danxi yaoyao buowen 或問), which, to judge from the date of its preface, was completed in 1384. $ ^{81} $

To understand how the Danxi corpus was formed, we need to identify the textual provenance of the statements found therein. Different levels of inter-textuality can be distinguished. “Interdiscursivity” is the unwitting yet inevitable “intertext” that literary critiques identify even in the works of the most original authors. $ ^{4} $ Some consider it to be constitutive of any discourse, spoken or written. “Manifest intertextuality” occurs when writers explicitly attribute a passage to another source. The Ming medical tradition was intertextual in both of these senses, but here I pay particular attention to a third and somewhat different kind of intertextuality, not only in the way texts deployed common cultural motifs or overtly referred to previous works, but in the very way texts were produced—intertextuality as a method for assembling texts. $ ^{5} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: The main the photolithographic reproduction of what may be its sole surviving copy: a 1648 Japanese edition kept at the Diet Library 内閣文庫 in Japan (RBXC: 247-70). A simplified-character edition based on the same copy appears in HWHG-12: 453-91. Like Wang Lun, Zhao was from Cixi 慈溪 (north of Ningbo), but his official career was less successful than Wang's, since he was cashiered in the early 1510s for either corruption or insubordination. In early 1510, Zhao had been promoted from magistrate of Yaozhou 姚州 in Yunnan to a higher post in Guangdong (Ming Wuzong 武宗 shilu, juan 57, p. 1277, Zhengde 4, 11th month, jiashen 甲申 day).

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: The diffusion of medical ideas took place in ways more complex than we can ever document. Knowledge and skills were transmitted through local teaching networks, some hereditary, others informal, and yet others based on strict ritual relations between a master and his (rarely her) disciples. $ ^{2} $ The vast majority of teachers remain undocumented. We know almost nothing about the routes by which medical books circulated throughout the empire, carried by traders, officials en route to their posts, or wandering physicians. The role of manuscripts and hand copies of printed books can only be surmised from notes made en passant in various writings, and the private letters that contributed to spreading new medical conceptions have almost all disappeared. $ ^{3} $ Finally we know too little about the oral teachings

History of the Ming (138: 4137) indicates that in the early Zhengtong reign (in or soon after 1436), Yang “dispatched military and civil officials to zhenfu Jiangxi, Huguang, Henan, and Shandong” (分遣文武鎮撫江西湖廣 河南 山東). Yang was probably the one who sent Chen to Shaanxi in 1435. John Dardess (1996: 179-89) discussed Yang's patronizing of natives of Taihe, but said little about his relations with other court officials. 72 LiuChYQ: 463. In 1438 Yang Shiqi had been named Junior Preceptor (少師), a prestige title ranked 1b. Duxian 都憲 is not listed in Hucker 1985, but it probably referred to a post in the Censorate. A sample mistake in Chen's edition: “Wang Andao” 王安道 (Zhu's disciple Wang Liu) was turned into “Yu'an daoren” 玉安道人 (“Taoist master Yu'an”; juan 1, “Wind strokes,” “neiyin si zhongfeng lun” 内因似中風論; LiuChYQ: 117A). 73 Angela Leung (2003b: 132) postulated that Yang's writing a preface for it showed that the Yijing xianxue 醫經小學 was “an important introductory textbook during the early Ming,” but it appears that Yang's preface enabled rather than recognized the influence of this text.

Zbezbong is difficult to translate. One of the earliest meanings of zbe 折 was “to break” or “to snap.” In legal language, zbe yu 折獄 meant “judging [i.e., deciding] a criminal case.” $ ^{6} $ But zbe in zbe yu also referred to the weighing and pondering that led to the final decision. $ ^{7} $ Zbe zbong (折中 or 折衷) itself implied the balanced assessment of various positions. $ ^{8} $ We may gloss zbezbong as to resolve—a case, a point of doctrine, etc.—by arbitrating between various positions, often on the basis of another authority. $ ^{9} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: “Danxi said…” The textual life of medical ideas on madness, 1360-1530 Danxi said that dian belongs to Yin and kuang to Yang, that dian is excessive joy and kuang excessive anger. He also stated that the doubling of Yin is dian and the doubling of Yang is kuang. 丹溪謂癲屬陰 狂屬陽 癲多喜 狂多怒 又云重陰者癲 重陽者狂 Xu Chunfu 徐春甫 in Gujin yitong daquan 古今醫統大全 (1556) $ ^{1} $ Chapters 3 and 4 traced a momentous transformation in the etiology of madness from Wind to internal Fire and mucus between the twelfth and the fourteenth century. The Ming dynasty (1368-1644) was not a period of strong epistemological breaks in medicine, but one in which physicians re-orchestrated earlier approaches to form new doctrinal and therapeutic emphases. Xu Chunfu above was citing the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法, a composite work compiled and printed in his own Huizhou Prefecture (Anhui) in the early 1480s and that quickly became the most influential work attributed to Zhu Zhenheng 朱震亨 ("Danxi" 丹溪: 1282-1358). This and the next chapter show how the development of Ming medical thinking on dian and kuang was shaped by three important trends that are well symbolized by Xu's quotation: the rising influence of precepts attributed to Danxi, the importance of Huizhou as a center of medical theorizing and publication, and the profound intertextuality of the elite medical tradition.

passages were bundled together in increasingly sophisticated sections on madness in the Danxi corpus, as well as in the works of the physicians who admired Danxi's works. For my argument to be convincing, I must therefore demonstrate the ascendancy of Danxi precepts. Discursive dominance requires a certain density of social diffusion. Through texts and people (notably Zhu's own disciples), Zhu Zhenheng's medicine first spread like a rhizome along arteries such as the Grand Canal, feeding on social connections and networks of teachers and readers, growing more densely in some places than others, and presenting different faces in the many areas where it flourished. The Danxi corpus became dominant insofar as many important debates between medical specialists concerned the way in which Danxi's legacy should be interpreted. Physicians appropriated the name "Danxi" as a symbol of authority or used it as a foil to make their own doctrinal emphases appear more distinctive. The diffusion of medical ideas took place in ways more complex than we can ever document. Knowledge and skills were transmitted through local teaching networks, some hereditary, others informal, and yet others based on strict ritual relations between a master and his (rarely her) disciples. $ ^{2} $ The vast majority of teachers remain undocumented. We know almost nothing about the routes by which medical books circ ...

that made texts meaningful in particular teaching traditions, and about the process by which practitioners developed their skills through a mix of readings, instruction by one or several masters or seniors, and interaction with real patients. All these caveats make our understanding of received texts all the more important. Physicians argued that mucus could stem from an almost infinite diversity of imbalances and failures to circulate. $ ^{18} $ Huang Yuanyu 黄元御 (1705-1758) grounded mucus in Dampness (associated with the Spleen), another common explanation. $ ^{19} $ Chen Xiuyuan 陳修園 (1753-1823), a popularizer of medical doctrines whose writings circulated widely in the second half of the nineteenth century, claimed that mucus was rooted in Water (the phase associated with either the two Kidneys or the left Kidney) and that sticky mucus appeared when Water Qi was “decocted” by excessive Yang (得陽煎熬). $ ^{20} $"

      "page_no": 289,
      "ocr_excerpt": "### 7.3 Shapes of practice: the scope of mucus therapies of madness The above section showed that mucus played an important role in Qing medical discussions of many illnesses. Starting with an examination of seventeenth-century medical primers that were popular under the Qing, let me now demonstrate from many different angles that mucus conceptions also dominated the way doctors thought about insanity. Since the practice of medicine was not regulated, virtually anyone could take it up. All that was needed was a medical primer and a few patients willing to pay for a cure. Physicians who sought a more elite clientele usually needed to claim a teacher and to be able to couch their arguments in classical terms drawn from the medical canons or the Yijing, but the success of medical primers suggests that medicine was easily learned outside of teacher-student relations. The large number of surviving editions of books like Huangfu Zhong's 皇甫中 Enlightened Physician Pointing to His Palm (Mingyi zhizhang 明醫指掌) or Gong Tingxian's Shoushi baoyuan 壽世保元, and the fact that other books lamented their excessive influence, suggest that they guided the practice of more physicians than the treatises mo ..."

cluster.chapter_title: Shapes of practices Medical trends surrounding dian and kuang, 1650-1911

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Qing Suzhou was an important center of medical studies supported by a prosperous printing industry and mature scholarly networks. As one of the main cities on the Grand Canal, it had long been an important hub of trade and communication, and many doctors had traveled there to find books and exchange ideas. But in Qing times Suzhou physicians claimed to represent the most advanced medical approaches in the empire. Although this pride predated the eighteenth century, it became particularly evident after the publication in 1766 of some of Ye Gui's 葉桂 (1667-1746; see 7.5 below) case files by men who claimed to be his disciples. I discuss Suzhou doctors' approach to madness in the next chapter. $ ^{10} $

with warming drugs, others kept blaming them for it, as when Xu Yuhe 許豫和 (late 18 $ ^{th} $ century) accused a physician of having exacerbated a madman’s symptoms with calorific replenishing, or when Wu Tang 吴塘 (1758-1836) claimed that another doctor’s attacking of mucus and Fire in a case of insanity had “helped the bandits do harm” (助賊為虐). $ ^{11} $

Although mucus and Fire had long been paired, Liang Xuemeng 梁學孟 in 1607 was perhaps the first to dedicate a section to “mucus Fire” (痰火) in a medical work (his Guoyi zongzhi 國醫宗旨). $ ^{12} $ His Tanbuo zhuanmen 痰火顓門 (1610) became the first work devoted entirely to this topic, $ ^{13} $ followed by Gong Juzhong’s 龔居中 Tanbuo dianxue 痰火點雪 (1630). Zhang Lu also discussed mucus-Fire in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通, 1695), explaining that “formful [i.e., visible and palpable] mucus” (有形之痰) was associated with “formless [invisible] Fire” (無形之火). $ ^{14} $ Cao Cunxin 曹存心 (1767-1834, from Changshu 常熟 in Jiangsu), later posed mucus and Fire as two equivalents: “Mucus is none other than Fire with form, whereas Fire is formless mucus” (痰即有形之火 火即無形之痰). $ ^{15} $

Although mucus and Fire had long been paired, Liang Xuemeng 梁學孟 in 1607 was perhaps the first to dedicate a section to “mucus Fire” (痰火) in a medical work (his Guoyi zongzhi 國醫宗旨). $ ^{12} $ His Tanbuo zhuanmen 痰火顓門 (1610) became the first work devoted entirely to this topic, $ ^{13} $ followed by Gong Juzhong’s 龔居中 Tanbuo dianxue 痰火點雪 (1630). Zhang Lu also discussed mucus-Fire in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通, 1695), explaining that “formful [i.e., visible and palpable] mucus” (有形之痰) was associated with “formless [invisible] Fire” (無形之火). $ ^{14} $ Cao Cunxin 曹存心 (1767-1834, from Changshu 常熟 in Jiangsu), later posed mucus and Fire as two equivalents: “Mucus is none other than Fire with form, whereas Fire is formless mucus” (痰即有形之火 火即無形之痰). $ ^{15} $

Although mucus and Fire had long been paired, Liang Xuemeng 梁學孟 in 1607 was perhaps the first to dedicate a section to “mucus Fire” (痰火) in a medical work (his Guoyi zongzhi 國醫宗旨). $ ^{12} $ His Tanbuo zhuanmen 痰火顓門 (1610) became the first work devoted entirely to this topic, $ ^{13} $ followed by Gong Juzhong’s 龔居中 Tanbuo dianxue 痰火點雪 (1630). Zhang Lu also discussed mucus-Fire in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通, 1695), explaining that “formful [i.e., visible and palpable] mucus” (有形之痰) was associated with “formless [invisible] Fire” (無形之火). $ ^{14} $ Cao Cunxin 曹存心 (1767-1834, from Changshu 常熟 in Jiangsu), later posed mucus and Fire as two equivalents: “Mucus is none other than Fire with form, whereas Fire is formless mucus” (痰即有形之火 火即無形之痰). $ ^{15} $

Although mucus and Fire had long been paired, Liang Xuemeng 梁學孟 in 1607 was perhaps the first to dedicate a section to “mucus Fire” (痰火) in a medical work (his Guoyi zongzhi 國醫宗旨). $ ^{12} $ His Tanbuo zhuanmen 痰火顓門 (1610) became the first work devoted entirely to this topic, $ ^{13} $ followed by Gong Juzhong’s 龔居中 Tanbuo dianxue 痰火點雪 (1630). Zhang Lu also discussed mucus-Fire in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通, 1695), explaining that “formful [i.e., visible and palpable] mucus” (有形之痰) was associated with “formless [invisible] Fire” (無形之火). $ ^{14} $ Cao Cunxin 曹存心 (1767-1834, from Changshu 常熟 in Jiangsu), later posed mucus and Fire as two equivalents: “Mucus is none other than Fire with form, whereas Fire is formless mucus” (痰即有形之火 火即無形之痰). $ ^{15} $

During the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, physicians increasingly asserted the general importance of mucus pathology. Zhang Lu claimed that mucus appeared in seven or eight ill people out of ten (痰飲為患 十人有七八), $ ^{16} $ whereas Cheng Guopeng 程國彭 (from She 歙 county in Huizhou; fl. early 18 $ ^{th} $ century) asserted that “There are no illnesses in which there is no fever or mucus” (凡病未有不發熱 不生痰者). $ ^{17} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: An interesting addition to these cases was co-editor Gong Shangnian's 龔商年 long elaboration on the symptoms, causes, and treatments of dian, kuang, and xian. This text (which I translate in Appendix 6) notably claimed that dian and kuang could both be caused by either depletion or over-repletion. $ ^{90} $ Before Gong, physicians had mostly classified dian as madness caused by depletion and kuang as more manic insanity occasioned by over-repletion.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: In both etiology and treatment, extant cases of “mad women” predating 1500 are indistinguishable from those of men. Xu Shuwei 許叔微 (1079-1154) gave the same pill to a woman who had been scared out of her wits by pirates and to a man who had lost his mind as he was about to be executed as a criminal. Wang Haogu 王好古 (13 $ ^{th} $ century) cured a manic woman with a purgative he borrowed from the Treatise on Cold Injury. $ ^{4} $ Two cases by Ni Weide 倪維德 (1303-1377) displayed the same gender neutrality. $ ^{5} $ If these physicians made distinctions between genders, they did not record them in their case files.

Zhu Zhenheng's 朱震亨 (1282-1358) influential view that possession symptoms were commonly triggered by mucus. $ ^{7} $ The third section tries to interpret the dramatic decrease in mentions of exorcistic healers in late-imperial cases that physicians or their patients' relatives explicitly attributed to "evil ghosts" or designated as "spectral affliction." I suggest that shamans and elite physicians no longer competed for the same patients. Yet, as I show in the fourth section, even in the late nineteenth century many elite physicians still ascribed illness to the action of various kinds of ghosts. The "medical exorcisms" they performed often borrowed from ritual therapies that today we associate more readily with Daoism than with scholarly medicine. $ ^{8} $ The final section explains how some physicians explained and treated hallucinations, a special type of possession symptom.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Medicine is the most complex field I shall be studying. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind. Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: ## 2 Prologue to Part One Diversity, coherence, and scheme transfer Medicine is the most complex field I shall be studying. Qing legislative and judicial practices were far from simple, but criminal trials followed common procedures and referred to a single body of laws that discussed madness and mad acts with consistent terminology. Although Qing laws were often modified, officials worked with only one code. Medical texts form a less cohesive corpus. They were produced in often-obscure contexts by authors or compilers who shared neither a common vocabulary nor a common understanding of madness. With no institution to stabilize it, the meaning of medical terms varied in time, between works of the same period, and even within individual works. $ ^{1} $ Unlike the relatively simple madness that the law described, medical kuang 狂 was characterized by a wide variety of behaviors and traced to origins as diverse as mucus accumulation in the chest, internally generated Liver Fire, and invasion by external Wind. Many contradictory explanations could even be presented as true in a single text. $ ^{2} $ To assess and explain this diversity properly, I pay attention not only to trendsetters and famous doctors, but also to writings that epitomized their age more than they marked it: $ ^{3} $ primers, florilegia of medical oddities, unpublished collections of medical cases, etc. In additio ...

inclusion of potent emetics in the court-sponsored Recipes of the Pharmacy Service (see p. 61 and 61n94 above), which was distributed to officials and eventually local physicians throughout the Song realm. Despite Chu’s warnings, the vomiting method remained a firmly established therapeutic practice and compilers of formularies continued to insert emetics for Wind strokes and other disorders into their books. Court compilers included the Suns’ “Saliva-thinning powder” in the Comprehensive Records from [the Hall of] Sagely Succor (Shengji zonglu 聖濟總錄; 1110/18), a gigantic imperial collection. $ ^{117} $ The same book recommended the use of “Chinese-quinine powder” (常山散) to induce vomiting in cases of intermittent fevers (niè瘧). $ ^{118} $ An emetic recommended in the Comprehensive Records was said to treat, among other symptoms, “crazy and disorderly language” (言語狂亂). Called Melon-pedicle powder from the name of the Shanghai lun recipe, it contained only melon pedicle (a recipe that was otherwise called “Lone-saint powder” 獨聖散). But the explanation of this recipe’s properties did not mention mucus. Other twelfth-century prescription manuals teemed with such emetics. A book that Zhang Congzheng (d. 1228) called “a recent Puji fang” (近代普濟方)—perhaps Zhang Rui’s Jifeng puji fang 雞峰普濟方 (1133)—recommended the emetics “Wind-chasing powder” (追風散) and “vomiting-Wind powder” (吐風散) to get ...

## Shapes of practice Medical trends surrounding dian and kuang, 17th-19th century The profusion of medical works that appeared after 1650 are an irritating windfall. As medical publications boomed, vectors of knowledge transmission and the doctrinal debates that interested physicians became more intricate and numerous. It thus becomes difficult to find narrative threads to explain how Qing medical reflections on madness changed. Far too many texts were published for me to base my conclusions on comprehensive data as I tried to do for earlier periods. But the extensive array of short treatises, collections of case files, formularies, and medical primers that appeared in Qing times hint to the thinking and practice of minor physicians who made no “contributions to the development of Chinese medicine”—to borrow a phrase from a less critical historiography. These works allow us to see how much medical luminaries had in common with their less famous counterparts. I first present a few important trends in Chinese medical thinking and practice from the seventeenth to the nineteenth century. After showing that the mucus etiology remained dominant over that period, I show how resilient and ubiquitous the script of blockage of the Heart by mucus was. I map the vivid “imagination of mucus” $ ^{1} $ through which physicians—from treatise writers to village healers—continued to envision ma ...

Xiuyuan 陳修園 (1753-1823) also adopted Zhang Lu's new interpretation of the “four masters” in his Three-Character Classic of Medicine (Yixue san zì jìng 醫學三字經; 1804), pointing out that Li Zhongzi's interpretation of them as including Zhang Ji was “erroneous” (誤). $ ^{9} $ ## Other Qing trends Qing Suzhou was an important center of medical studies supported by a prosperous printing industry and mature scholarly networks. As one of the main cities on the Grand Canal, it had long been an important hub of trade and communication, and many doctors had traveled there to find books and exchange ideas. But in Qing times Suzhou physicians claimed to represent the most advanced medical approaches in the empire. Although this pride predated the eighteenth century, it became particularly evident after the publication in 1766 of some of Ye Gui's 葉桂 (1667-1746; see 7.5 below) case files by men who claimed to be his disciples. I discuss Suzhou doctors' approach to madness in the next chapter. $ ^{10} $ One trend in the eighteenth and especially the nineteenth century was the rising number of case collections in the surviving medical literature. Many of them were never printed. They allow us to continue comparing the propositions found in treatises with the way physicians actually handled mad patients. Through them, we can also understand better how women were treated when they behaved in ways t ...

with warming drugs, others kept blaming them for it, as when Xu Yuhe 許豫和 (late 18 $ ^{th} $ century) accused a physician of having exacerbated a madman’s symptoms with calorific replenishing, or when Wu Tang 吴塘 (1758-1836) claimed that another doctor’s attacking of mucus and Fire in a case of insanity had “helped the bandits do harm” (助賊為虐). $ ^{11} $ Medical writing also remained profoundly intertextual. Several treatises from the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries included long and increasingly complex sections on madness. They were longer and denser partly because they could borrow from a larger number of texts, but also because they applied to more disorders than ever the old claim that any illness could be caused by multiple factors. Two representative examples are Feng Zhaozhang's 馮兆張 (from Haiyan 海鹽 in Zhejiang) Jinnang milu zazheng daxiao becan 錦囊祕錄雜證大小合參 (in 20 juan; 1694) and Shen Jin'ao's 沈金鰲 (1717-1776; from Wuxi 無錫 in Jiangsu) Zabing yuanliu xizhu 雜病源流犀燭 (in 30 juan; 1773), both of which concentrated on explaining miscellaneous illnesses. Besides these exhaustive accounts of manifold etiologies, we find attempts to simplify—even unify—medical doctrines, books with titles like Unity in the Forest of Medicine (Luo Denggao's 駱登高 Yilin yizhi 醫林一致; 1703), Knowing Medicine in One Glance (Zhu Shijin's 朱時進 Yijian nengyi 一見能醫; 1769), and Medicine for Sots (Long Huitang's ...

### 7.2 The continued dominance of the mucus etiology In earlier chapters, I examined the rise of mucus in Chinese medical thinking and its eventual use in explanations of madness. I also showed how the Danxi corpus had given pride of place to the mucus etiology in general. On the basis of these now mature theories, seventeenth-century physicians continued to discuss how mucus emerged, what disorders it caused, and how these illnesses should be treated. Some did so in special sections on this viscous intruder, others in commentaries on the notion of “mucus and rheum” (痰飲; 3.5) in Zhang Ji’s Essentials of the Golden Casket (3 $ ^{rd} $ century CE). Although mucus and Fire had long been paired, Liang Xuemeng 梁學孟 in 1607 was perhaps the first to dedicate a section to “mucus Fire” (痰火) in a medical work (his Guoyi zongzhi 國醫宗旨). $ ^{12} $ His Tanbuo zhuanmen 痰火顓門 (1610) became the first work devoted entirely to this topic, $ ^{13} $ followed by Gong Juzhong’s 龔居中 Tanbuo dianxue 痰火點雪 (1630). Zhang Lu also discussed mucus-Fire in his Comprehensive Medicine (Yitong 醫通, 1695), explaining that “formful [i.e., visible and palpable] mucus” (有形之痰) was associated with “formless [invisible] Fire” (無形之火). $ ^{14} $ Cao Cunxin 曹存心 (1767-1834, from Changshu 常熟 in Jiangsu), later posed mucus and Fire as two equivalents: “Mucus is none other than Fire with form, whereas Fire is formless mucus” (痰 ...

During the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, physicians increasingly asserted the general importance of mucus pathology. Zhang Lu claimed that mucus appeared in seven or eight ill people out of ten (痰飲為患 十人有七八), $ ^{16} $ whereas Cheng Guopeng 程國彭 (from She 歙 county in Huizhou; fl. early 18 $ ^{th} $ century) asserted that “There are no illnesses in which there is no fever or mucus” (凡病未有不發熱 不生痰者). $ ^{17} $ This perceived ubiquity of mucus generated more explanations of its pathological origins. Physicians argued that mucus could stem from an almost infinite diversity of imbalances and failures to circulate. $ ^{18} $ Huang Yuanyu 黄元御 (1705-1758) grounded mucus in Dampness (associated with the Spleen), another common explanation. $ ^{19} $ Chen Xiuyuan 陳修園 (1753-1823), a popularizer of medical doctrines whose writings circulated widely in the second half of the nineteenth century, claimed that mucus was rooted in Water (the phase associated with either the two Kidneys or the left Kidney) and that sticky mucus appeared when Water Qi was “decocted” by excessive Yang (得陽煎熬). $ ^{20} $

### 7.3 Shapes of practice: the scope of mucus therapies of madness The above section showed that mucus played an important role in Qing medical discussions of many illnesses. Starting with an examination of seventeenth-century medical primers that were popular under the Qing, let me now demonstrate from many different angles that mucus conceptions also dominated the way doctors thought about insanity. Since the practice of medicine was not regulated, virtually anyone could take it up. All that was needed was a medical primer and a few patients willing to pay for a cure. Physicians who sought a more elite clientele usually needed to claim a teacher and to be able to couch their arguments in classical terms drawn from the medical canons or the Yijing, but the success of medical primers suggests that medicine was easily learned outside of teacher-student relations. The large number of surviving editions of books like Huangfu Zhong's 皇甫中 Enlightened Physician Pointing to His Palm (Mingyi zhizhang 明醫指掌) or Gong Tingxian's Shoushi baoyuan 壽世保元, and the fact that other books lamented their excessive influence, suggest that they guided the practice of more physicians than the treatises modern historians rely on in their studies of Chinese medical thinking. $ ^{21} $ ## Shapers of practice? request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

Finally I describe the replenishing therapies that other contemporary physicians deployed to treat madness symptoms. Both in his approach to madness and in his general doctrinal discussions, Wang often tackled issues that were discussed in the contemporary medical literature. The larger community of experts of which he was part tackled issues defined by the Danxi corpus (see Chapter 5) and discussed the writings of illustrious authors like Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510; see 5.6). In his work on poxes (Douzhi libian 痘治理辨; preface from 1531), Wan ..."

      "page_no": 233,
      "ocr_excerpt": "earliest case collections in Chinese medical history, $ ^{25} $ but it also served Wang Ji to make an array of doctrinal points on pathology and human vitality, and to defend his singular clinical approaches. The most distinctive and controversial aspect of Wang's medical practice was his use of calorific drugs (chiefly ginseng and astragalus) to treat cases of depletion that other physicians (following Wang Lun) would have treated with coolants. $ ^{26} $ Wang Lun had attributed visible emissions of Blood to \"Fire\" resulting from Blood/Yin deficiency, and argued that warming drugs only exacerbated this destructive Fire, and should therefore be proscribed when treating such cases (5.6). Strongly opposing this admonition, Wang Ji tried to show that calorific replenishers could treat not only cases of Qi depletion (as everyone agreed), but also illnesses that were caused by Blood depletion. Wang Ji had to dispute Wang Lun’s authority in his daily medical practice. Not only did rival physicians routinely use coolants to replenish Yin and Blood, it appears that even patients cited Wang Lun’s Miscellaneous Writings of Enlightened Physicians (1502) to challenge Wang Ji’s prescriptions. $ ..."

cluster.chapter_title: Replenish or attack? Competing etiologies and therapies of madness, 1530-1650

unmatched_anchors.source_text: It is no coincidence that Zhu Zhenheng's disciples and admirers were the leading transmitters of this flexible art of prescription during the early Ming dynasty. Zhu's own case files played a role. Wang Xing's 王行 preface (dated 1377, less than 20 years after Zhu's death) to a compendium of Zhu's cases explained how unusual Zhu's approach had been. $ ^{9} $ Most physicians, Wang claimed, instructed "to treat such-and-such an illness with this remedy, and that such-and-such a remedy treats such-and-such an illness" (治某病以某方以某方治某病而已). But "illnesses change greatly and are without constancy, whereas recipes are fixed and limited" (病多變而無常 方一定而有限). And how could one
“respond to inconstant illnesses with limited recipes”(以有限之方應無常之病)? $ ^{10} $ Wang
received from Dai himself the cases by Zhu that Wang Xing was prefacing. $ ^{11} $

The success of other practitioners in the “Zhu Danxi” line was also explained by their distinctive refusal to stick to set recipes. Wang Xing attributed the “extraordinary efficacy” (奇驗) of Dai Sigong’s cures to his adoption of Danxi’s flexible methods. According to one of Jiang Wusheng’s 蔣武生 (1351-1424) biographers, Jiang “did not hold to the old recipes, but investigated the root of illnesses and made his own recipes; therefore his treatments were always successful” (不執古方而究病所本 自為方 故所治恆十全). $ ^{12} $ The spread of case records would be difficult to understand without the notion that every instance of illness deserved a unique treatment, and without the prestige associated with this flexible style of prescription.

“respond to inconstant illnesses with limited recipes”(以有限之方應無常之病)? $ ^{10} $ Wang Xing attributed these singular doctrines (殊不類常聞也) to the aptly styled Wang Lifang 王 立方 (Wang “Establish-a-prescription”), who owed his distinctive ideas to having been taught by Zhu’s pupil Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405; see 5.3). Wang Lifang had allegedly received from Dai himself the cases by Zhu that Wang Xing was prefacing. $ ^{11} $

“respond to inconstant illnesses with limited recipes”(以有限之方應無常之病)? $ ^{10} $ Wang Xing attributed these singular doctrines (殊不類常聞也) to the aptly styled Wang Lifang 王 立方 (Wang “Establish-a-prescription”), who owed his distinctive ideas to having been taught by Zhu’s pupil Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405; see 5.3). Wang Lifang had allegedly received from Dai himself the cases by Zhu that Wang Xing was prefacing. $ ^{11} $

collections were actually published in the sixteenth century, and all three of them were the work of men from Huizhou in Anhui. Apart from Wang Ji's book (6.2), I know only of the Categorized Case Files of Illustrious Physicians (Mingyi Leian 名醫類案 [1591], a florilegium of cases by many doctors) $ ^{13} $ and of Sun Yikui's 孫一奎 large collection (6.6), which his disciples collated in the 1590s. $ ^{14} $ Sun was himself a second-generation disciple of Wang Ji. Both Wang and Sun had recorded their cures long before they had planned to publish them, and published them too late in their lives to compete for clients. $ ^{15} $ And since the editors of the Categorized Case Files did not include cases of their own in their compilation, they could not have published this book to attract customers. Since only two men – both from Huizhou and in the same intellectual lineage – published a collection of their own cases in the sixteenth century, no wave of yian was unleashed that would require a sociological explanation. $ ^{16} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: A notorious adage circulating in late imperial medical circles declared that the women's illnesses were more difficult to cure than men's. $ ^{1} $ Two other sayings claimed that women's bodies were "governed by Blood" (以血為主 or a variant) and that treating women necessitated "separate prescriptions" (别方). Ming and Qing physicians cited these sayings to set up concurring arguments, to introduce objections or qualifications, or to clinch points with the help of a well-formulated piece of common knowledge.

strong replenishers of Blood and Qi and treat other illness symptoms as secondary." 73 Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法, opening lines of sec. 92, "Postpartum" 產後, in juan 5 (Danxi yiji: 304). "Dispersing the surface" referred to induced sweating, a therapy designed to expel excessive Heat. Like many "Danxi" pronouncements, this one was assembled in the fifteenth century from fragments that had also appeared in the Jingui gouxuan 金匱鉤玄 (late 14th century) and its likely predecessor, a manuscript that was published in 1475 under the title Danxi michuan fangjue 丹溪密傳方訣. See Jingui gouxuan, juan 1, "Shui zhong" 水腫 (Danxi yiji: 99; this passage does not appear in the SKQS ed.): "after childbirth, one must chiefly use massive replenishing of Blood and Qi" (產後必用大補氣血為主); and 3.11b, "Furen ke" 婦人科, "Fa re wu han" 發熱惡寒 (Danxi yiji: 122): "peonies cannot be given postpartum because their acidity and cooling [properties] destroy vital Qi. Dispersing must not be used [to treat] any postpartum disorder." (產後不可下白芍以其酸寒伐生發之氣故也 產後一切病皆不可發散) The first editor of the Danxi xinfa in the 1450s, a man named Yang Xun 楊珣, compiled these passages in his effort to reconstruct Zhu Zhenheng's approach to postpartum problems. See Yang's Danxi xinfa, sec. 102, "Postpartum" 產後, in juan 4 (Liu and Xue 2005: 301). More on the Xinfa and its editions in 5.5. Earlier remarks by Liu Chun 劉純 contained a key difference from the wording of the Danxi xinfa. "Our Danxi xiansheng said that if there are no noxious blockers (如無惡阻) postpartum, one should massively replenish Qi and Blood, and that even if there are other symptoms, they should be treated as offshoots" (Yuji weiji 玉機微義 [1396] 49.58a [LiuYChQ: 436B]. Liu probably copied these instructions from notes or manuscripts, since some of these phrases appeared in Danxi michuan fangjue 丹溪秘傳方訣 (HWHG 5: 210-13). It could be that Yang Xun, who was from Xi'an, read the 1439 edition of Liu's Yuji weiji (which was also made in Xi'an), but his version of Danxi's claims on postpartum disorders was nonetheless cruder than Liu's. The Huizhou editors of the Xinfa simply recopied the resulting sentence from Yang's recension. Zhu's more complex instructions on how a woman who had just given birth should be treated (from Jufang fabui 局方發揮 1.28b; Danxi yiji: 46) were rarely cited. An exception was Lou Ying's Yixue gangmu 醫學綱目 [1396, first printed in 1565] (juan 35, "Chanhou zheng" 產後癥; 1996 [1396]: 811A).

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: The emergence of case files as a distinct genre partly stemmed from this flexible conception of illness. Yet even the best English-language histories of Chinese case records usually leap straight from Xu Shuwei to Wang Ji's Stone-Mountain's Medical Case Files (Shishan yian 石山醫案 [1531]; see 6.2) without discussing the dominance of this conception of illness and style of prescription among Jin and Yuan physicians. $ ^{3} $ This omission introduces inaccuracies in accounts of the development of “medical case files” and obscures the process by which they emerged as an independent genre in the sixteenth century.

An entry in the madness section of the anonymous Yifang biannan dacheng 醫方辨難大成 (1850) was devoted to possession (juan 92, “Diankuang biannan” 顛狂辨難, “Are some manic symptoms caused by harm done by evil spirits?” 狂證果有邪祟為害否; 2005 [1850]: 534-35).

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: Most elite physicians from the twelfth century onward, while recognizing that there was “constancy” (chang 常) to illnesses, emphasized that the best healers should also react to “transformations” (bian 變), the events that diverged from the regular ways in which reality was supposed to unfold. “Constancy” itself referred not to unchanging essences but to the regular and predictable way in which processes developed. The emergence of case files as a distinct genre partly stemmed from this flexible conception of illness. Yet even the best English-language histories of Chinese case records usually leap straight from Xu Shuwei to Wang Ji's Stone-Mountain's Medical Case Files (Shishan yian 石山醫案 [1531]; see 6.2) without discussing the dominance of this conception of illness and style of prescription among Jin and Yuan physicians. $ ^{3} $ This omission introduces inaccuracies in accounts of the development of “medical case files” and obscures the process by which they emerged as an independent genre in the sixteenth century. First, despite many claims to the contrary, Wang Ji's work was not the first autonomous work to contain the cases of a single physician. $ ^{4} $ Zhang Congzheng's Ten Configurations and Three Therapies (Shixing sanliao 十形三療), an anthology of Zhang's cases, was edited soon after his death (1228) by his disciples, and was circulating on its own by the fourteenth cen ...

Dai Liang 戴良 and the author of his epitaph Song Lian 宋濂 concurred that Zhu kept detailed records of his clinical encounters. As Dai explained in a text written in 1358 or 1359: For the illnesses [Zhu] treated, what the illness looked like, what excellent remedy he applied, and the drinking of what drugs led to healing [were recorded]. From the past until now, for the efficacious [cases]: who [his patient] was, from what village in what county [he or she came], the names of the interested party, and what [Zhu] had witnessed and heard are clearly documented. $ ^{7} $ 其所治病凡機病之狀何如 施何良方 飲何藥而愈 自前至今 驗者何人 何縣里 主名 得諸見聞 班班可紀 Song Lian even claimed that Zhu's "disciples possess books [of these records], classified by illnesses" (門人類證有書). $ ^{8} $ It is no coincidence that Zhu Zhenheng's disciples and admirers were the leading transmitters of this flexible art of prescription during the early Ming dynasty. Zhu's own case files played a role. Wang Xing's 王行 preface (dated 1377, less than 20 years after Zhu's death) to a compendium of Zhu's cases explained how unusual Zhu's approach had been. $ ^{9} $ Most physicians, Wang claimed, instructed "to treat such-and-such an illness with this remedy, and that such-and-such a remedy treats such-and-such an illness" (治某病以某方以某方治某病而已). But "illnesses change greatly and are without constancy, whereas recipes are fixed and limited" (病多變而無常 方一定而有限). And h ...

“respond to inconstant illnesses with limited recipes”(以有限之方應無常之病)? $ ^{10} $ Wang Xing attributed these singular doctrines (殊不類常聞也) to the aptly styled Wang Lifang 王 立方 (Wang “Establish-a-prescription”), who owed his distinctive ideas to having been taught by Zhu’s pupil Dai Sigong 戴思恭 (1324-1405; see 5.3). Wang Lifang had allegedly received from Dai himself the cases by Zhu that Wang Xing was prefacing. $ ^{11} $ The success of other practitioners in the “Zhu Danxi” line was also explained by their distinctive refusal to stick to set recipes. Wang Xing attributed the “extraordinary efficacy” (奇驗) of Dai Sigong’s cures to his adoption of Danxi’s flexible methods. According to one of Jiang Wusheng’s 蔣武生 (1351-1424) biographers, Jiang “did not hold to the old recipes, but investigated the root of illnesses and made his own recipes; therefore his treatments were always successful” (不執古方而究病所本 自為方 故所治恆十全). $ ^{12} $ The spread of case records would be difficult to understand without the notion that every instance of illness deserved a unique treatment, and without the prestige associated with this flexible style of prescription. Christopher Cullen’s argument (2001) that case collections started to flourish as a printed genre in the sixteenth century because an increasing number of educated men sought distinctive ways to foster their medical careers after they had failed the civil e ...

collections were actually published in the sixteenth century, and all three of them were the work of men from Huizhou in Anhui. Apart from Wang Ji's book (6.2), I know only of the Categorized Case Files of Illustrious Physicians (Mingyi Leian 名醫類案 [1591], a florilegium of cases by many doctors) $ ^{13} $ and of Sun Yikui's 孫一奎 large collection (6.6), which his disciples collated in the 1590s. $ ^{14} $ Sun was himself a second-generation disciple of Wang Ji. Both Wang and Sun had recorded their cures long before they had planned to publish them, and published them too late in their lives to compete for clients. $ ^{15} $ And since the editors of the Categorized Case Files did not include cases of their own in their compilation, they could not have published this book to attract customers. Since only two men – both from Huizhou and in the same intellectual lineage – published a collection of their own cases in the sixteenth century, no wave of yian was unleashed that would require a sociological explanation. $ ^{16} $ Before we inscribe the “rise of case files” within broad social trends, it may be more prudent to understand the motivations of the individuals who published such works, and to situate their case records in the broader context of their other medical works and of their contemporaries’ use of case files. Stone Mountain’s Medical Case Files was one of as many as eleve ...

which had enjoyed a strong reputation for medical studies since at least the Song, was where the prominent third edition of the Danxi xinfa 丹溪心法 had been published in 1482 and a sequel called Danxi xinfa fuyu 丹溪心法附語 appeared in 1536. In 1556, Huizhou man Xu Chunfu 徐春甫 completed a compendium called Gujin yitong daquan 古今醫統大全, a mosaic of short texts or excerpts, and had it printed in Suzhou the following year. $ ^{17} $ Two editions of Wu Kun's 吴崑 Yifang kao 醫方考 (with a 1584 preface by Wu) were also printed in 1586, whereas Sun Yikui's works came out in the 1580s and 1590s. These Huizhou physicians were supported by a strong intellectual tradition, a network of active printers, and a growing commercial wealth that facilitated the sponsoring of medical books. $ ^{18} $ If we stop focusing on autonomous case collections, we also realize that Wang Ji was not the only man of his time to record his clinical encounters. Han Mao 韓懋 (fl. 1520s), Wu Qiu 吳球 (fl. 1520-30s), Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559), and certainly many others whose names are no longer remembered also did. $ ^{19} $ Although these men did not publish their own collections, their records circulated among specialists. Xue Ji interspersed his editions of earlier texts with cases of his own. $ ^{20} $ The practice of writing case files was thus fairly widespread in the most prosperous region of the empire. Wang Ji's decision to pub ...

shall see in the next section, Wang Ji, just like both his predecessors (Zhu Zhenheng) and his contemporaries (Xue Ji), used his cases to support broader medical arguments. I would say the “case files” genre emerged because an increasing number of doctors kept records of their clinical encounters, both for their patients and for their own reference. Their record-keeping depended on the notion that each bout of illness potentially required a unique therapy. It would thus seem that the sixteenth-century “bureaucratization” of case files that Cullen documented—and the common likening of medical intervention to judicial reasoning and judgment—was simply the repackaging along new metaphorical lines of a kind of record that had become increasingly important since approximately the thirteenth century, when some elite physicians adopted the flexible conception of illness I just mentioned. $ ^{21} $ Charlotte Furth (2007a: 19) has interpreted the case form (in medicine as well as other fields) as a “strategy of modernists in an intellectual tradition that gave awesome weight to the authority of antiquity,” and medical cases as tools to “negotiate the gap between… medical canon and therapeutic strategy.” $ ^{22} $ Without downplaying the freedom allowed by case files to go beyond canonical formulations, I would contend that, at least in the sixteenth century when they came into widesprea ...

### 6.2 Replenishing madness: Wang Ji's case-based reasoning Wang Ji 汪機 (1463-1540), from Qimen 祁門 county in Huizhou, was one of a handful of sixteenth-century physicians who attributed a case of mad behavior to depletion and treated it accordingly. $ ^{23} $ This section outlines the emergence, in the works of Wang and a few more doctors, of a conception of madness caused by depletion that would become more prominent later in the sixteenth century. I first explain the doctrinal background of Wang's predilection for treating patients with ginseng and astragalus root (黄芪), then I present two of his madness cases in light of his broader orientations. Finally I describe the replenishing therapies that other contemporary physicians deployed to treat madness symptoms. Both in his approach to madness and in his general doctrinal discussions, Wang often tackled issues that were discussed in the contemporary medical literature. The larger community of experts of which he was part tackled issues defined by the Danxi corpus (see Chapter 5) and discussed the writings of illustrious authors like Wang Lun 王綸 (1453-1510; see 5.6). In his work on poxes (Douzhi libian 痘治理辨; preface from 1531), Wang Ji had addressed Wei Zhi's 衛直 recently published Boai xinjian 博愛心鑒 (1525), whereas Wang's work on "external medicine" (Waike lili 外科理例; 1531 preface) integrated arguments he had found in Waike fabui ...

earliest case collections in Chinese medical history, $ ^{25} $ but it also served Wang Ji to make an array of doctrinal points on pathology and human vitality, and to defend his singular clinical approaches. The most distinctive and controversial aspect of Wang's medical practice was his use of calorific drugs (chiefly ginseng and astragalus) to treat cases of depletion that other physicians (following Wang Lun) would have treated with coolants. $ ^{26} $ Wang Lun had attributed visible emissions of Blood to "Fire" resulting from Blood/Yin deficiency, and argued that warming drugs only exacerbated this destructive Fire, and should therefore be proscribed when treating such cases (5.6). Strongly opposing this admonition, Wang Ji tried to show that calorific replenishers could treat not only cases of Qi depletion (as everyone agreed), but also illnesses that were caused by Blood depletion. Wang Ji had to dispute Wang Lun’s authority in his daily medical practice. Not only did rival physicians routinely use coolants to replenish Yin and Blood, it appears that even patients cited Wang Lun’s Miscellaneous Writings of Enlightened Physicians (1502) to challenge Wang Ji’s prescriptions. $ ^{27} $ When he disagreed with a rival’s diagnosis in front of patients and their relatives, Wang Ji sometimes argued that loss of blood, a symptom commonly attributed to Yin/Blood depletion, was in ...

In the three remaining killings, which were all discussed in a single statute, death was understood as being inflicted involuntarily. $ ^{13} $ “Killing in play” (戲殺) occurred in the course of dangerous activities in which people engaged purposely, like sword-practice. $ ^{14} $ This rare crime was punished by “strangulation after the Assizes,” but classified as “suspended” (緩決) at the Assizes, resulting in an automatic commutation to exile at 3,000 li. “Strangulation after the Assizes” was also the basic sentence for “mistakenly killing” (誤殺) a bystander while fighting somebody else. Since this crime was committed during a deliberate act of violence, the sentence was adjusted depending on the gravity of the act during which the lethal
mischance had occurred, or, as commentator Shen Zhiqi 沈之奇 specified in the early eighteenth century, on the intention that motivated the act that led to the mistaken killing. $ ^{15} $

“Accidental killing” (過失殺) could be redeemed by conferring 12.42 ounces of silver to the family of the victim to provide for burial fees. $ ^{16} $ This punishment was mild because the law recognized that the culprit had lacked any will to kill, harm, or even partake in dangerous activities. Compensation for a life taken was paid with money, not with one’s own life. Since “Killing because of madness” in the early Qing was sentenced in the same way as “accidental killing,” let me cite the entire text of the relevant statute. $ ^{17} $

Anyone who kills or injures another accidentally $ ^{18} $ (this is comparatively less serious than killing in play) will be sentenced as if for killing or injuring in an affray. [The privilege of] monetary redemption will be granted according to law, and the money will be given to the family (of the person who was killed or injured). (Accident means that which the ear or eye does not extend to, or what was not contemplated. rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Xu San 徐三, son of Xu Jiuwei 徐九緯, was a “hunter” (打牲人) under the supervision of Palace eunuch Shen Maode 沈茂德. “Hunters” (Manchu: butha; Ch.: buteba 布特哈) was the generic name given to several non-Manchu tribes in an area that is now at the confines of Inner Mongolia and Heilongjiang in northern China. $ ^{23} $ One day with a knife Xu San stabbed (戳) his father, who died shortly thereafter, but before he died, the father had time to testify that “his son had been suffering from madness illness” (伊子原有瘋疾). The Ministry of Punishments approved the sentence that an original memorial (now lost) had proposed: Xu San was exempted of any crime (免罪) because he was judged to have killed “because of madness illness” (因瘋疾). This decision had no other legal repercussions because it was not established as a precedent for judging future cases of a similar nature. $ ^{24} $

of madness” was integrated into the network of the six killings. $ ^{30} $ From then on, and until the end of the Qing, killings committed in a fit of insanity were legally viewed as lacking both awareness and criminal intention.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: After determining that Jia had hit his wife in anger and intentionally set his house afire, the magistrate had to select the most fitting penal denomination. "Killing three junior relatives in the same family who are not [guilty of a crime that deserves] the death penalty" $ ^{47} $ (殺期服卑幼一家非死罪三人) did not apply, because neither Jia's wife nor his elder daughter had died from his hands. A more suitable law was: "Whenever one intentionally sets fire and burns the house of an official or a commoner, and thereby kills or injures someone, sentence like 'intentional killing'" 凡放火故燒官民房屋者 致殺人者 以故殺論. $ ^{48} $ Killing one's wife intentionally was punished by "strangulation after the Assizes."

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Just as someone who had suffered from madness could be punished severely if his acts were considered intentional, the law also distinguished mad acts from acts committed by simpletons. Like Jia Chandou, Jia Yicai 叚一才, who also lived in the Yongzheng period (1723-1735), had suffered from fits of insanity, during which he would go crazy and run around wildly (病發起來就痴痴癲癲 胡走亂跑了).⁵⁰ When his fits stopped occurring for good, his father released him from confinement (從先原是鎖禁的 後因他不瘋了纔放開了), but by then Jia had become "stupid by nature" (賦性愍獸). He spent most of his time with the children of his small village (有些愍頭愍腦的 好與村裡娃娃們頑耍). An eight-year-old boy often called him a wangba 忘八 or an idiot (你是個獃子), and insulted him by saying that his five-year-old daughter was someone else's (你的女兒是別人養的).⁵¹ Having heard these words, Jia Yicai started suspecting that his wife had engaged in lecherous behavior (淫行). Many times, he cornered the boy and asked him to explain where he had heard this rumor, but the child would not respond.
