rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Even when depositions recorded people’s voices accurately, however, they did not necessarily reflect what commoners would have said about the people under trial if they had been interacting with them or talking about them in a daily context. Since we have no access to the words that the witnesses actually pronounced, the best we can do is to assess each memorial on an individual basis while keeping in mind all the caveats I have just outlined. $ ^{7} $

This being said, the language of reports suggests that madness could be discerned in one’s facial expression or countenance (神色). rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: 就得了痰迷病症). $ ^{52} $ Han Chun (1816) was 48 sui and worked as a hired laborer when his young daughter died in 1814. His grief was so profound that it turned into madness (悲痛過切 得了瘋迷病症). $ ^{53} $ Lin Zhigong 林志功 started acting strangely after his wife and young son both died of illness in 1735. $ ^{54} $ Jia Chandou's son died in 1730. As his father explained, Chandou's son "had poxes and died. [The] Qi [of grief] confused his heart and he got a feng [madness] illness" (出痘死了 氣迷了心 就得了一個瘋病). $ ^{55} $ In 1760, a few years after Chai Shijin's 柴世進 wife had died, his only surviving son fell into a river and drowned. He "missed his son so much that [his grief] turned into madness illness" (他想念兒子 致成瘋病). $ ^{56} $ Liu Yuhou's 劉裕後 father and younger brother made a living operating a wooden raft on the Yellow River (在黃河運放木筏營生). In 1738 their raft was crushed by the current and they drowned. Liu, who could not even find the bodies, cried day and night until he "got madness illness, with intermittent fits" (得了瘋病 時發時止). $ ^{57} $

In contrast with medical texts, which usually focused on organic processes, most people cited in legal reports attributed madness to an extreme emotional experience. Some became crazy from fright, Men Fa 門發 (1816) because he had almost drowned in his buffaloes' water pit, Bai Shengqi 白升奇 (1751) because his boat had capsized. $ ^{50} $ A physician was said to have gone mad because other doctors had mocked his failures. $ ^{51} $ Many reported falling ill because they had lost a young child. Bannerman Jixiang (1816) became crazy in 1815 soon after his son was killed by the collapse of a wall in the Beijing house where they lived: "I often missed my son, then I got mucus-confusion disorder" (我時常想念兒子

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: The magistrate composed a summary of the entire affair and proposed a temporary sentence according to the relevant laws. He sent his report to his immediate superior the prefect, along with the formal depositions (招狀) of the criminal and of all the witnesses, reconstituted from the clerk's notes. I have already discussed the several reviews this preliminary sentencing had to go through before it could be confirmed (see 10). The depositions we read in these provincial reports were thus several times removed from the words that the parties had originally uttered. Yasuhiko Karasawa (2007) has noticed broad discrepancies between draft depositions and the way they were cited in later reports. $ ^{5} $ He has shown that during the course of the Qing the legal secretaries who composed the final drafts of tiben increasingly tended to edit testimonies so as to confirm the magistrate’s (and the governor’s) findings, removing from the depositions lies, smokescreens, gaps or contradictions in evidence, and all the possibly truthful details that did not appear in the official narrative of the case (which in turn purported to be based on the confessions). In some madness cases, all the witnesses were cited as having used the same words in the same order to describe what happened on the day of a crime. Even the killer used the same words despite claiming that he had no memory of what had happ ...

Depositions were edited translations of orality rather than direct transcriptions. Conventions of legal reporting such as the use of the written style and the selective editing of depositions make it difficult to assess what terms common people used to describe madness. Very specific depositions and those that “sound oral” were probably closer to the original words of the witnesses than the conventionally formulated ones. The memorials that quoted the witnesses as having said different things with different words in two different trial sessions are also more likely to have been authentic. The fact that these depositions consistently sounded more oral seems to confirm this view. It remains possible, however, that in many cases some seemingly oral usage reflected a secretary’s use of a vernacular style of writing as a device to enhance the authenticity of the depositions. Even when depositions recorded people’s voices accurately, however, they did not necessarily reflect what commoners would have said about the people under trial if they had been interacting with them or talking about them in a daily context. Since we have no access to the words that the witnesses actually pronounced, the best we can do is to assess each memorial on an individual basis while keeping in mind all the caveats I have just outlined. $ ^{7} $ ## The interest of the formulaic The expressions concerning ...

the reports. Interestingly, all made their way into depositions. $ ^{8} $ Bureaucratic jargon is particularly important because it appeared everywhere. Only good knowledge of legal texts and the reading of numerous tiben allow to separate the formulaic from words that the witnesses would have been more likely to recognize as their own. Before I turn to the depositions themselves, let me survey the main set phrases that punctuated legal reports and how we can use them to some profit despite their seemingly opaque uniformity. The concepts we see in documents were more than “background noise” that should be reduced before we can get to the undistorted facts. These objects were precisely those that institutions and practitioners purported to discuss and act upon. Formulaic phrases may have given little idea of what the person they described looked and sounded like, but they spoke eloquently of what the legal field expected madness to sound and look like. Judicial reports almost invariably noted that the eyes of killers who had just been arrested “were staring straight” (兩目直瞪 or 兩目直視) and that their “words were incoherent” (語無論次). These words described the stereotypically stupefied-and-rambling madman who could not provide the deposition that was necessary for sentencing to take place. Since these reports were written only after painstaking investigations had confirmed that the pers ...

recognized by these outward signs would be putting the result (an ex post facto report phrased in unequivocal terms) before the process of identification. This being said, the language of reports suggests that madness could be discerned in one’s facial expression or countenance (神色). The visage of many mad men was described as “confused” (神色昏迷). $ ^{11} $ By contrast, the aspect and language of a recovered person were said to be “sober” or “clear” (清爽). $ ^{12} $ A longer set phrase specified that the recovered killer’s “facial expression was sober and his speech clear” (神清色爽 語言明晰). $ ^{13} $ Since officials knew that legal madness was all or nothing: the reports they wrote therefore made as strong a distinction as possible between the normal and the mad: a dumb-struck expression contrasted with a sober one; incoherent speech with clear language. Tiben often cast the events that had led to a killing into the predetermined script of how the law expected madness cases to unfold. Because most mad killings were committed during sudden fits and because the law defined madness as utter unawareness of one's own actions, the vast majority of memorials specified that on such-and-such a date, the would-be killer "got madness illness, with intermittent fits; when it flares up, he is unaware of people and things" (得了瘋病 時發時止 發時不醒人事). $ ^{14} $ This was how the prototypical scenario of a mad ...

Since only absolute insanity was legally recognized, it is possible that many people whom trials determined were mad probably looked the way set phrases described them. But these formulaic expressions were meant to attest more than to describe. By establishing a seamless correspondence between specific events and a general scenario, officials used these phrases to make the choice of the penal denomination “killing because of madness” appear as indisputable as possible. This “fool-proofing” of judicial sentences was important, because officials could be imposed administrative sanctions if they sentenced a crime too lightly, too heavily, or according to the wrong legal article. Concrete observations funneled into a few turns of phrase aimed to convince reviewers that the madness of the “criminal” under trial was indeed as extreme as the law mandated. $ ^{15} $ The criminal's recovery from his incoherence made it possible to extract a confession. In most reports from the late Qing, when testimonies were rewritten to confirm the final verdict, few words were the criminal's own. The killer and most of the witnesses were reported to have said about the same thing in about the same words. The killer insisted that he "did not know how" (不知怎樣), he had grabbed an object – a club, a vegetable knife (菜刀, very common), a carrying pole, a staff, an axe, a sickle, a stool, a cane, a knife to ...

The confession was not an admission of guilt doubled with repentance (though depositions sometimes contained statements of regret, dismay, or contrition), but the killer's formulation of the crime for which he would be sentenced—his act as it was characterized by the law. Since by definition he did not remember his acts, his confession could only be the ratification of the version of the crime that officials had settled upon after investigation. Medical case files also contained formulaic turns of phrase, but they tended to be more flexible, perhaps because they recorded symptoms that physicians had observed (by contrast with both writers of medical treatises, who were trying to present general arguments, and legal officials or secretaries, who had not witnessed the acts of the people they were describing) and because these symptoms could be important to the establishment of a therapy. $ ^{16} $ The readers of medical case files also differed from those of legal reports. Whereas legal reports were meant to be assessed by superiors or equals, medical records were written for the relatives of patients or for readers of collections of case files. Although physicians occasionally used formulaic phrases (often descriptions of symptoms drawn from the Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon) in their case files, they had nothing to gain from channeling all their cases into the same convenient mo ...

affliction by nine different terms. $ ^{17} $ Putting aside those related to “mucus” for the moment, they were “madness illness” (the official term for madness), fengbing 風病 (lit. “Wind illness,” but homophonous with the term that meant “madness illness”), fengdian 風顛 (“madness”; lit. Wind dian), $ ^{18} $ fengkuang 風狂 (“madness”; lit. Wind kuang), diankuang 顛狂 (“insanity”), $ ^{19} $ fengkuang bing 風狂病 (“madness illness”), fengkuang bingzheng 風狂病症 (“madness disorder”), and fengdian bingzheng 風顛病症 (“madness disorder”). $ ^{20} $ We cannot assume that people used the term “madness illness” orally. Considering that fengdian and fengkuang were intelligible on their own and were widely used in non-judicial contexts, we should probably interpret the compounds fengkuang bing, fengkuang bingzheng, and fengdian bingzheng—which were clearly not oral—as bureaucratic jargon. The suffixes bing and bingzheng may have been used to make clear that the law considered madness to be an illness. $ ^{21} $ Apart from the pair-turned-compound diankuang, all the above terms contain the sound feng. A clerk or official who wanted to transcribe the sound feng could choose between the homophonous 瘋 and 風. The fact that the report on Deng Tingmei’s case used 瘋 only when quoting the letter of the law suggests that the 瘋 usage was still marginal at the time. $ ^{22} $ The last two “terms for madness” emplo ...

(“Wind-mucus blocking the openings [of the Heart]”). In accord with medical conceptions I have already analyzed, tanmi xinqiao identified mucus as the cause of madness and the Heart as the seat of cognitive and perceptive activities. $ ^{23} $ “Wind-mucus blocking the openings” was a variant specifying that mucus had been caused by Wind. $ ^{24} $ This Wind etiology, which had become exceptional in Qing medicine, was reflected in the way the report explained the onset of Deng’s madness. Whereas most memorials said that the would-be killer “got madness” (得了瘋病), the 1736 memorial claimed Deng “contracted” (run 染) it, a term usually reserved for illnesses caught by contact with some kind of pathogenic Qi. $ ^{25} $ Although not all these terms for madness were technical medical categories, most of them had equivalents in the medical literature. The use of medically acceptable terminology by commoners and legal specialists alike does not imply that these people embraced medical conceptions. It confirms instead that the same set of terms could accommodate many different conceptions. The non-technical medical words used in legal depositions differed from expressions like “Liver stasis” (肝鬱), which referred to a specifically medical understanding of pathology rather than to a general kind of behavior like fengkuang. $ ^{26} $ In the same 1736 memorial, several people described the ons ...

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

      "ocr_excerpt": "Some of the doubts that officials expressed about the madness (or “besottedness”) of text bearers probably resulted from bureaucratic necessity. cluster.chapter_title: 15. Madmen talking? Symbolic subversion in eighteenth-century trials

unmatched_anchors.source_text: his friends and shop-keeping neighbors in the 1740s and 1750s until he came to the attention of the government. Wang Qi 王琦 made enormous efforts to find an official who would accept his brother Xun's 珣 text until an illiterate military official agreed to receive it. $ ^{7} $ In the rest of this chapter, I examine the writing practices of men who were not classically literate but shared dreams of achieving something through writing. Belief in the power of writing can be observed both in their naive hope that a text would bring rewards when presented to imperial administrators, and in the often frenzied reaction of officials to these text-bearers.

As many of the men I discuss in this chapter, Chai Shilu's brother Shijin 世進 came to official attention while trying to present a text to an imperial administrator. Donations of letters and texts were not always illegal, but the Qing code contained a law that forbade the presentation of texts in order to gain recognition or employment: seeking advancement under a false pretense was to be punished with 100 strokes of the heavy stick. $ ^{9} $

Despite the heavy punishment incurred, all sorts of people presented texts to officials to attract their attention. Many of the cases I discuss in this chapter commenced in a similar fashion, when “mad people” contacted the officialdom to voice grievances, to express praise for the emperor, or to seek advice or employment. $ ^{10} $ Education commissioners’ (學證) constant travels, their proximity to licentiates, and their moral reputation made them favorite targets for grievance bearers and letter writers. $ ^{11} $

Despite the heavy punishment incurred, all sorts of people presented texts to officials to attract their attention. Many of the cases I discuss in this chapter commenced in a similar fashion, when “mad people” contacted the officialdom to voice grievances, to express praise for the emperor, or to seek advice or employment. $ ^{10} $ Education commissioners’ (學證) constant travels, their proximity to licentiates, and their moral reputation made them favorite targets for grievance bearers and letter writers. $ ^{11} $

Offerings of dubious texts were matters serious enough to be discussed in palace memorials, a form of document designed for reporting on important state matters. Governors and Governors-general usually conducted interrogations in person and were responsible for mobilizing lower officials for swift and thorough investigations. Officials scrutinized the family background and network of relations of the “criminals,” their possible involvement in unlawful activities, and whether they were concealing more “illicit writings.” $ ^{12} $

He was sentenced to death by beating, a heavy punishment considering that officials admitted he did it “without awareness”(無知).¹⁴ The use of this irregular punishment indicates that Wang’s “crime” was considered serious enough to justify disregard for penal denominations, but that it was not completely on a par with treason or sedition, which would have led to the execution of all of Wang’s male relatives and the enslavement of his female kin.

There is some vision in what is reported in Chen Hongmou's memorial requesting the locking up of mad people. rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: An even more recent wave of scholarship on wenziyu has by and large retained the view that the Qing indiscriminately repressed young Chinese thinkers and intellectuals. $ ^{6} $ Anecdotal accounts and emphasis on the unfairness of the condemnations are still the chief aspects of this scholarship. Now that the anti-Manchu sentiment has disappeared (since the Manchus are now retrospectively presented as a “minority nationality” within the broad Chinese nation), the fault for wenziyu is rejected on “feudal government” in general.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: In any case, as we saw in the case of Liang Nianquan, the fact that someone was recognized as crazy did not make his words any less seditious. Da ni (“sedition” or “great defiance”), the crime for which Ding was executed, was one of the “Ten abominations” (十惡). Ni did not mean putting together a full-scale rebellion; $ ^{51} $ it meant “going against,” with the additional sense of going in the wrong direction, against the flow of a river, for instance. Ding’s writings were transgressive not in the sense of having “gone too far,” but of having “gone against.” He “went against” because he played on the same symbolic field as the emperor. Ding was not seen as an inoffensive parody of imperial symbolic prestige, because he challenged the reigning dynasty by recuperating its main symbols of legitimacy. But these “public symbols” were for public view, not for public use.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: At first sight, it would appear that the “boundary” between madness and sedition was somewhat “blurred” in these eighteenth-century trials. This formulation rightly conveys that it was never clear what constituted mad speech, but it runs into the awkward problem of why people whom officials explicitly recognized as mad were still executed for sedition. $ ^{52} $ Indeed, Qing officials were not interested in determining whether someone was either crazy or seditious: one could be both at the same time, equally clearly.

A flurry of articles on that subject appeared in the first year of publication of several academic or semi-academic journals in 1979 and 1980, soon after the Third Plenum of the Eleventh Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party in late 1978 had confirmed the fall of the Gang of Four and modified conditions of scholarship. $ ^{5} $ An even more recent wave of scholarship on wenziyu has by and large retained the view that the Qing indiscriminately repressed young Chinese thinkers and intellectuals. $ ^{6} $ Anecdotal accounts and emphasis on the unfairness of the condemnations are still the chief aspects of this scholarship. Now that the anti-Manchu sentiment has disappeared (since the Manchus are now retrospectively presented as a “minority nationality” within the broad Chinese nation), the fault for wenziyu is rejected on “feudal government” in general. There may be issues that partisan debates and anecdotal accounts have kept us from noticing in these trials. What kinds of words or writings were considered seditious enough to deserve death even as those who uttered them were recognized as crazy? What type of political regime could feel threatened by such words? In what contexts were expressions of loyalty unacceptable? Are we being misled by what we feel is a clear boundary between madness and crime? I seek answers to these questions in four interrelated domains ...

Wang Qi 王琦 made enormous efforts to find an official who would accept his brother Xun's 珣 text until an illiterate military official agreed to receive it. $ ^{7} $ In the rest of this chapter, I examine the writing practices of men who were not classically literate but shared dreams of achieving something through writing. Belief in the power of writing can be observed both in their naive hope that a text would bring rewards when presented to imperial administrators, and in the often frenzied reaction of officials to these text-bearers. Donations of letters and texts were not always illegal, but the Qing code contained a law that forbade the presentation of texts in order to gain recognition or employment: seeking advancement under a false pretense was to be punished with 100 strokes of the heavy stick. $ ^{9} $

Despite the heavy punishment incurred, all sorts of people presented texts to officials to attract their attention. Many of the cases I discuss in this chapter commenced in a similar fashion, when “mad people” contacted the officialdom to voice grievances, to express praise for the emperor, or to seek advice or employment. $ ^{10} $ Education commissioners’ (學證) constant travels, their proximity to licentiates, and their moral reputation made them favorite targets for grievance bearers and letter writers. $ ^{11} $ Offerings of dubious texts were matters serious enough to be discussed in palace memorials, a form of document designed for reporting on important state matters. Governors and Governors-general usually conducted interrogations in person and were responsible for mobilizing lower officials for swift and thorough investigations. Officials scrutinized the family background and network of relations of the “criminals,” their possible involvement in unlawful activities, and whether they were concealing more “illicit writings.” $ ^{12} $ Official suspicion about these texts contrasted with the hopes of those who presented them. He was sentenced to death by beating, a heavy punishment considering that officials admitted he did it “without awareness”(無知).¹⁴ The use of this irregular punishment indicates that Wang’s “crime” was considered serious enough to justify disregard for penal denominations, but that it was not completely on a par with treason or sedition, which would have led to the execution of all of Wang’s male relatives and the enslavement of his female kin. They should also be denied access to writing implements. We know of Chen’s memorial through an appreciative edict by the Qianlong emperor: $ ^{16} $ There is some vision in what is reported in Chen Hongmou's memorial requesting the locking up of mad people. Some of the doubts that officials expressed about the madness (or “besottedness”) of text bearers probably resulted from bureaucratic necessity. When reporting on a case, they could not assume that one who looked or sounded crazy was crazy. This “institutionalized strategy” (Bourdieu 1990: 138) on the part of officials was the object of no specific rule, but it was strongly encouraged in bureaucratic practice. Those officials who embodied this fear of mad-sounding speech were praised, whereas officials who simply assumed that the authors of these texts were mad were punished if their negligence was later discovered. $ ^{25} $ But even when officials had found no illegal activities behind a suspicious text, they were not necessarily clement. request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

governor-general, who, after another review (usually made by his legal secretaries), transcribed all the evidence into a tiben 題本, a “routine memorial” that he sent to the Grand Secretariat (Neige 內閣), which forwarded it to the Ministry of Punishments for final confirmation. $ ^{6} $ In a new memorial, the “Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingke 刑科) sometimes rejected (bo 駁), but usually confirmed the sentence proposed by the governor. $ ^{7} $ These “routine memorials” recounted entire incidents—from before the killing to the arrest—and the way they were processed by the judicial system. They even cited depositions from physicians or the relatives of mad killers, and even from the very men who were under trial. $ ^{8} $ Much of Chapters 13 to 15 are based on these depositions.

As has become customary in English-language scholarship, I will refer to Qing legal articles by their numbers in Huang Jingjia's 黄靜嘉 1970 re-edition of Xue Yunsheng's 薛允升 Lingering Doubts in the Study of the Sub-Statutes (Duli cunyi 讀例存疑; 1905). Xue's work was originally not an edition of the code, but an unpublished critical commentary on the "sub-statutes" (例) as they existed after 1870, when the Qing Code had last been overhauled. $ ^{9} $

Whereas in English law acts committed because of insanity did not count as crimes, Chinese imperial law sentenced killings committed because of madness differently depending on the circumstances. $ ^{10} $ For the larger part of the Han dynasty (206 BCE – 220 CE), people who killed in a state of insanity were sentenced to death. Only in the early second century CE
were mad killers granted an unspecified reduction of punishment (狂易殺人 待減重論). $ ^{11} $

No law concerning mad homicides existed during the Tang dynasty (617-908). Instead, a statute called “Those Who Are Aged, Juvenile, or Disabled” (老少廢疾) granted limited clemency to “critically disabled” 篤疾 people (including mad people 癲狂) who committed crimes. When mad persons committed a homicide that deserved the death penalty, their sentence (decapitation or strangulation) had to be set forth in a special memorial to the emperor to petition for his decision. $ ^{12} $ How the emperor treated such cases is unknown. The Song dynasty (960-1279) adopted the Tang approach wholesale. $ ^{13} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: # 10 ## Prologue to Part Two Madness and mad people in the Qing dynasty have been studied mostly in a legal context. $ ^{1} $ The easy availability and relative intelligibility of legal documents vis-à-vis medical ones have undoubtedly contributed to this trend. $ ^{2} $ Following pioneers like Chen Hsiu-fen and Angelika Messner, I showed in Part One that Qing medical approaches to madness were also a legitimate topic. In Part Two, I want to show that the study of Qing legislative and judicial activities surrounding madness can be improved, and gains from being compared with the many other ways in which mad acts, words, and people were handled in both technical and non-technical contexts. In this prologue and the next few chapters, I will often propose brief comparisons with the medical field, leaving more systematic reflections for Chapter 16, the general conclusion. But before I turn to comparisons, let me first explain how the Qing legal field functioned and what kinds of documents it produced. Doing so will help to understand how the object "madness illness" (feng bing 瘋病) emerged from concrete legal practices. I then present the ways in which dynasties prior to the Qing dealt with homicides committed in a fit of insanity. $ ^{3} $ Then I probe the origin of the term "madness illness" by which Qing law

governor-general, who, after another review (usually made by his legal secretaries), transcribed all the evidence into a tiben 題本, a “routine memorial” that he sent to the Grand Secretariat (Neige 內閣), which forwarded it to the Ministry of Punishments for final confirmation. $ ^{6} $ In a new memorial, the “Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingke 刑科) sometimes rejected (bo 駁), but usually confirmed the sentence proposed by the governor. $ ^{7} $ These “routine memorials” recounted entire incidents—from before the killing to the arrest—and the way they were processed by the judicial system. They even cited depositions from physicians or the relatives of mad killers, and even from the very men who were under trial. $ ^{8} $ Much of Chapters 13 to 15 are based on these depositions. But routine memorials have limits as sources of information. Since they were written only after a sentence had been meted out, the information they presented was designed to lead seamlessly to the proposed verdict. These highly structured reports left no room for debate, unless an official from the Ministry of Punishments had raised a point that required debate. When doubts were expressed, it was usually to show that they had already been resolved. The limits of these documents depend on the information we seek from them. They may provide little information on the daily life of “ordin ...

Created in the late seventeenth century, palace memorials exploded in numbers after the creation of the Grand Council during the Yongzheng reign (1723-1735). The Grand Council, which unlike the Grand Secretariat worked in direct collaboration with the monarch, became the most important agency in the central government from the 1730s until the end of the Qing. Legal cases judged grave (like parricide or sedition) had to be reported to the Grand Council by palace memorial. Such cases were processed more quickly than those reported through routine memorials, because palace memorials were considered more urgent, and because the sentences meted out in them necessitated no repeated reviews. Unlike tiben, palace memorials could also contain documentary attachments, such as seized texts or more detailed transcriptions of depositions. The cases I discuss in Chapters 14 and 15 were all brought to the attention of the throne through palace memorials. Besides “routine” and “palace” memorials, I also found information on Qing official views of madness in successive editions of the Qing Code (called Dai Qing liü 大清律, Dai Qing liü jijie fuli 大清律集解附例, or Dai Qing lüli 大清律例 depending on when they were published), commentaries on the Code, imperial edicts, memorials on policy issues, as well as several collections of legal cases. As has become customary in English-language scholarship, I will re ...

Lingering Doubts contained neither the diagrams (on family relations, legal instruments of punishment, technical terms, table of monetary redemption, etc.) included at the beginning of the official editions of the Code, nor the running commentaries that topped each page in both private and state-sponsored editions of the Code. It is Huang Jingjia's inclusion of these tables and his numbering of all the statutes and sub-statutes that make his edition (not a "reprint") of Lingering Doubts so convenient. Xue's analyses themselves are invaluable for tracing the origin of most sub-statutes, though he was imprecise about Kangxi-era (1662-1722) legislation. ## The pre-Qing handling of mad homicides Let me now discuss pre-Qing legal treatments of mad homicides and trace at the same time the emergence of the character feng 瘋, by which Qing law officially referred to insanity. The terms by which "madness" was designated in Chinese imperial law changed through time, yet no word in particular implied either distance from or closeness with medical approaches. The legal treatment of mad people under dynasties that used medical terms like diankuang 顛狂 and fengkuang 風狂 seems to have been no more (or less) compatible with medical approaches than that of eras that used less medical concepts like xinfeng 心風 or kuangyi 狂易. Whereas in English law acts committed because of insanity did not count as ...

were mad killers granted an unspecified reduction of punishment (狂易殺人 待減重論). $ ^{11} $ No law concerning mad homicides existed during the Tang dynasty (617-908). Instead, a statute called “Those Who Are Aged, Juvenile, or Disabled” (老少廢疾) granted limited clemency to “critically disabled” 篤疾 people (including mad people 癲狂) who committed crimes. When mad persons committed a homicide that deserved the death penalty, their sentence (decapitation or strangulation) had to be set forth in a special memorial to the emperor to petition for his decision. $ ^{12} $ How the emperor treated such cases is unknown. The Song dynasty (960-1279) adopted the Tang approach wholesale. $ ^{13} $

Unlike the Tang and Song, the Yuan dynasty (1260-1368) promulgated several explicit laws concerning homicides committed by mad people. Yuan courts were instructed to diminish the sentence of mad killers, but not to absolve them from penalties entirely. For instance, a man who killed one of his parents or grand-parents in a state of insanity (風狂) was to be simply “executed” (處死) rather than put to death by dismemberment. $ ^{14} $ In 1269 a man killed four people in a fit of “Heart Wind” (心風). $ ^{15} $ Legal officials petitioned his case to the emperor (上請) “in accordance with the old precedent” (照依舊例) that instructed to do so in cases of killing because of insanity (顛狂殺人). $ ^{16} $ Khubilai Khan eventually absolved the killer from the death penalty by imperial edict (敕裁) and condemned him to paying 50 ounces of silver (per person?) in “cremation or burial fees” (燒埋銀) to the family of his victims, the same amount as for people who committed a regular homicide. $ ^{17} $ Khubilai's reasoning may have been the same as the one behind another Yuan law stating that someone who, in a state of madness (病風狂), injured another person during a fight, leading to that person's death (毆傷人致死), should be "absolved of crime" (免罪),

though cremation or burial fees should still be collected (徵燒埋銀) from the culprit (History of the Yuan 105: 2678). Early Qing laws handled mad homicides in a similar fashion, though the legal reasoning that justified monetary compensation differed. $ ^{18} $ Another Yuan law absolved those who “spoke inappropriately” (失口亂言) as long as they had done so while “suffering from Wind madness” (害風狂疾) and if there was nothing else behind their words. $ ^{19} $ As I will show in Chapters 14 and 15, the Qing attitude toward seditious words pronounced by mad people would be much harsher. Yuan legal documents referred to madness as xin feng 心風 (“Heart Wind”), feng kuang 風狂 (“Wind kuang”), dian kuang 顛狂 (“insanity”), and perhaps even feng bing 瘋病, the term that Qing law would officially adopt to discuss insanity. $ ^{20} $ Despite their different origins, these names did not have different legal implications. $ ^{21} $

The key to the widening use of feng 瘋 to mean “madness” was its phonetic equivalence with feng 風 (“Wind”) and the age-old association of the sound feng with madness in expressions like fengkuang (風狂, “Wind madness”), fengdian (風癲, “Wind dian”), and xin feng 心風 (“Heart Wind”). By the twelfth century the sound feng (“Wind”) had been associated with madness for so long that it had come to mean madness even when it was not followed by either kuang or dian nor disambiguated by the addition of the “illness radical” (疒) to yield the form 瘋. $ ^{22} $ One “crazy monk brother” (風僧哥) was mentioned in a twelfth-century collection of anecdotes. $ ^{23} $ The term fa feng 發風 (“fit of Wind” = “fit of madness”) also appeared in a Yuan play titled A Pair of Battling Quacks (Shuang dou yi 雙鬥醫). $ ^{24} $ As mainstream medicine stopped attributing madness to Wind, $ ^{25} $ feng in the above compounds became an etymological reminder rather than an etiological marker. In other words, the term fengkuang no longer implied a belief that madness was caused by Wind, just as “lunatic” no longer refers to people who have lost their mind because of the moon. $ ^{26} $ In the late seventeenth century, for example, a physician from Huizhou in Anhui explained in his medical treatise that kuang people were often called fengzi 風子, a term that became widely used in Qing times, though it tended to be written 瘋子 ...

cluster.chapter_title: 16. Madness multiple Language, illness, and behavior in legal and medical practice

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Was madness a single object, then? And were words like diankuang and jengbing all synonyms? No. Although both legal and medical specialists counted on the easy recognizability of madness, they transposed daily practice and local labels into specialized categories and scripts. $ ^{2} $ Penal denominations and illness names channeled phenomena into understandable and manageable forms. Physicians, for instance, tended to reduce to organic imbalances some problems that had probably emerged through social interactions. They asserted their competence by claiming to understand the organic roots of emotional discomforts and behavioral disorders. This reductionism created—or “enacted”—a different kind of madness than the one found in ritual therapies or judicial practice. $ ^{3} $ As such, physicians faced a different object than legal officials.

Was madness a single object, then? And were words like diankuang and jengbing all synonyms? No. Although both legal and medical specialists counted on the easy recognizability of madness, they transposed daily practice and local labels into specialized categories and scripts. $ ^{2} $ Penal denominations and illness names channeled phenomena into understandable and manageable forms. Physicians, for instance, tended to reduce to organic imbalances some problems that had probably emerged through social interactions. They asserted their competence by claiming to understand the organic roots of emotional discomforts and behavioral disorders. This reductionism created—or “enacted”—a different kind of madness than the one found in ritual therapies or judicial practice. $ ^{3} $ As such, physicians faced a different object than legal officials.

Legal officials did not understand madness in the same way as physicians. Although the legal object “madness illness” borrowed from medical and popular conceptions, it evolved to suit the concerns and categories of the legal field. At first, Qing law targeted only one kind of act (homicides), not madness or mad people in general. A killing was considered to have been committed “because of madness” when the killer had had no cognizance—and had no recollection—of his act. $ ^{4} $ This lack of awareness was what convinced early Qing officials that mad killers should not be physically punished. $ ^{5} $

Legal officials did not understand madness in the same way as physicians. Although the legal object “madness illness” borrowed from medical and popular conceptions, it evolved to suit the concerns and categories of the legal field. At first, Qing law targeted only one kind of act (homicides), not madness or mad people in general. A killing was considered to have been committed “because of madness” when the killer had had no cognizance—and had no recollection—of his act. $ ^{4} $ This lack of awareness was what convinced early Qing officials that mad killers should not be physically punished. $ ^{5} $

Fiction could be put to many more uses. One feature of mad people in medical texts and popular perception was the peculiarity of their words. How did fictional sources portray mad speech? My impression is that mad characters in Chinese stories were usually misrecognized as mad before being revealed to be something else—playful immortals in disguise, hermits or visionaries, or people in the grip of real demons. Madness thus served to dissimulate something else, sometimes from the reader, but most often from other characters in the story. $ ^{6} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: task. Yet case records remind us that illness meanings were more than organic. As an affliction that was defined socially, insanity was always more than a bodily event. $ ^{6} $ Categories and scripts channeled phenomena into intelligible and manageable forms; they also guided perception and suggested methods to tackle the reality they purported to describe. Broadly speaking, medical specialists attributed mad symptoms either to invasion by external forces (“deviant Qi”, Wind, ghosts) or to internal imbalances strong enough to provoke illness. $ ^{7} $ Doctors claimed they could cure their patients by expelling these external invaders or redressing these pathogenic imbalances. Because of this emphasis on dynamic situations and a general therapeutic optimism, the medical tradition considered permanent disorders anomalous. Chinese physicians therefore rarely medicalized personality and almost never called patients “madmen” (狂人 or 瘋子). The medical scripts by which doctors understood madness changed significantly between the eighth and the fourteenth century, and again from the fourteenth to the sixteenth century. I plan to demonstrate that new explanations and treatments for insanity often borrowed from the prototypical scenarios of other illnesses or from contemporaneous interpretations of human vitality rather than from the observation of mad patients.
